abstract: Large policies pretrained on a combination of Internet-scale vision-language data and diverse robot demonstrations have the potential to change how we teach robots new skills: rather than training new behaviors from scratch, we can fine-tune such vision-language-action (VLA) models to obtain robust, generalizable policies for visuomotor control. Yet, widespread adoption of VLAs for robotics has been challenging as 1) existing VLAs are largely closed and inaccessible to the public, and 2) prior work fails to explore methods for efficiently fine-tuning VLAs for new tasks, a key component for adoption. Addressing these challenges, we introduce OpenVLA, a 7B-parameter open-source VLA trained on a diverse collection of 970k real-world robot demonstrations. OpenVLA builds on a Llama 2 language model combined with a visual encoder that fuses pretrained features from DINOv2 and SigLIP. As a product of the added data diversity and new model components, OpenVLA demonstrates strong results for generalist manipulation, outperforming closed models such as RT-2-X (55B) by 16.5% in absolute task success rate across 29 tasks and multiple robot embodiments, with 7x fewer parameters. We further show that we can effectively fine-tune OpenVLA for new settings, with especially strong generalization results in multi-task environments involving multiple objects and strong language grounding abilities, and outperform expressive from-scratch imitation learning methods such as Diffusion Policy by 20.4%. We also explore compute efficiency; as a separate contribution, we show that OpenVLA can be fine-tuned on consumer GPUs via modern low-rank adaptation methods and served efficiently via quantization without a hit to downstream success rate. Finally, we release model checkpoints, fine-tuning notebooks, and our PyTorch codebase with built-in support for training VLAs at scale on Open X-Embodiment datasets.

keyInsights: Fine-tuning the vision encoder is crucial for achieving high performance in robot control, suggesting pretrained vision backbones need adaptation for precise spatial details.
Extensive training over many epochs (27) with a fixed learning rate is essential for maximizing action token accuracy and real-world robot performance.

Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) and 4-bit quantization enable significant reductions in computational requirements and memory footprint, making VLA model adaptation and deployment practical on commodity hardware without substantial performance degradation, provided vision encoder adaptation is included.
originalProblem: Traditional robotic manipulation policies were task-specific and struggled with generalization to novel objects, scenes, or unseen instructions.
Existing state-of-the-art Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models were largely closed-source, limiting accessibility, transparency, and collaborative community development.
There was a lack of established, efficient methods for fine-tuning large VLA models to new robot embodiments and tasks, particularly for deployment on more accessible, commodity hardware.
results: OpenVLA, a 7B-parameter model, established a new state of the art for generalist robot manipulation, outperforming the 55B-parameter RT-2-X by 16.5% absolute success rate across 29 tasks on WidowX and Google Robot embodiments.

The model demonstrated effective and data-efficient adaptation to new real-world robot setups with small datasets (10-150 demonstrations), outperforming from-scratch imitation learning methods by 20.4% on diverse multi-instruction tasks.

LoRA fine-tuning achieved performance comparable to full fine-tuning while training only 1.4% of parameters, reducing compute by 8x, and 4-bit quantization maintained performance while halving GPU memory footprint to 7.0 GB for inference at ~6Hz on consumer GPUs.
solution: Develops and releases OpenVLA, a 7B-parameter, fully open-source Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model based on a Llama 2 LLM combined with a fused DINOv2 and SigLIP visual encoder.
Trains OpenVLA on 970k real-world robot demonstrations from the Open X-Embodiment dataset, formulating robot action prediction as a next-token prediction task by tokenizing continuous actions.

Investigates and validates efficient fine-tuning methodologies, including parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) and 4-bit quantization, to enable practical adaptation and inference on commodity hardware.
paper_summary.summary: OpenVLA introduces a fully open-source, 7B-parameter Vision-Language-Action model that sets a new state of the art for generalist robot manipulation, outperforming larger closed-source models by 16.5% absolute success rate. The model also demonstrates effective and efficient fine-tuning strategies for adapting to new robot setups and tasks on commodity hardware.

title: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model

abstract:
Recent vision-language-action models (VLAs) build upon pretrained
vision-language models and leverage diverse robot datasets to demonstrate
strong task execution, language following ability, and semantic generalization.
Despite these successes, VLAs struggle with novel robot setups and require
fine-tuning to achieve good performance, yet how to most effectively fine-tune
them is unclear given many possible strategies. In this work, we study key VLA
adaptation design choices such as different action decoding schemes, action
representations, and learning objectives for fine-tuning, using OpenVLA as our
representative base model. Our empirical analysis informs an Optimized
Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe that integrates parallel decoding, action chunking, a
continuous action representation, and a simple L1 regression-based learning
objective to altogether improve inference efficiency, policy performance, and
flexibility in the model's input-output specifications. We propose OpenVLA-OFT,
an instantiation of this recipe, which sets a new state of the art on the
LIBERO simulation benchmark, significantly boosting OpenVLA's average success
rate across four task suites from 76.5% to 97.1% while increasing action
generation throughput by 26$\times$. In real-world evaluations, our fine-tuning
recipe enables OpenVLA to successfully execute dexterous, high-frequency
control tasks on a bimanual ALOHA robot and outperform other VLAs ($\pi_0$ and
RDT-1B) fine-tuned using their default recipes, as well as strong imitation
learning policies trained from scratch (Diffusion Policy and ACT) by up to 15%
(absolute) in average success rate. We release code for OFT and pretrained
model checkpoints at this https URL

keyInsights: Combining parallel decoding with action chunking drastically improves both VLA inference efficiency and policy performance, increasing OpenVLA's success rate by 14% on LIBERO.
Using continuous action representations with a simple L1 regression objective yields comparable task performance to diffusion-based methods while offering significantly faster training and inference.
A well-designed fine-tuning strategy enables VLAs pretrained on single-arm data to successfully adapt and even outperform models with bimanual pretraining on complex bimanual manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often have slow inference speeds (e.g., 3-5 Hz), making them unsuitable for high-frequency robotic control (25-50+ Hz) required for dexterous manipulation.
Current VLA fine-tuning methods show unsatisfactory performance on complex tasks, particularly in bimanual manipulation scenarios.
There is a lack of clear, empirically-validated guidance on how to effectively fine-tune VLAs for optimal speed and success on novel robot setups and tasks.
results: OpenVLA-OFT achieved a new state of the art on the LIBERO simulation benchmark with an average success rate of 97.1% (up from 76.5%) and a 26x speedup in action generation throughput (109.7 Hz).

On a real ALOHA bimanual robot, OpenVLA-OFT+ successfully executed diverse dexterous tasks, consistently outperforming other fine-tuned VLAs and achieving up to 15% higher average success rates, despite being pretrained only on single-arm data.
The inclusion of Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) was essential for robust language grounding on the ALOHA robot, preventing language-dependent task success from plummeting to chance levels.
solution: A systematic empirical analysis was conducted on OpenVLA, evaluating key design choices for VLA fine-tuning: action generation strategy (autoregressive vs. parallel decoding with action chunking), action representation (discrete vs. continuous), and learning objective (L1 regression vs. diffusion).

An "Optimized Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe" was developed, integrating parallel decoding, action chunking, continuous action representation, and a simple L1 regression objective for efficient and high-performance VLA adaptation.

Architectural modifications were introduced to support multiple camera views and low-dimensional robot state inputs, with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) added to the OFT+ variant for enhanced language grounding.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Stanford University developed an Optimized Fine-Tuning (OFT) recipe for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, substantially increasing their inference speed and task success on new robotic setups, including complex bimanual manipulation, by integrating parallel decoding, action chunking, and L1 regression with continuous actions.

title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models: Optimizing Speed and Success

abstract:
Vision-language models (VLMs) pretrained on large-scale multimodal datasets
encode rich visual and linguistic knowledge, making them a strong foundation
for robotics. Rather than training robotic policies from scratch, recent
approaches adapt VLMs into vision-language-action (VLA) models that enable
natural language-driven perception and control. However, existing VLAs are
typically massive--often with billions of parameters--leading to high training
costs and limited real-world deployability. Moreover, they rely on academic and
industrial datasets, overlooking the growing availability of
community-collected data from affordable robotic platforms. In this work, we
present SmolVLA, a small, efficient, and community-driven VLA that drastically
reduces both training and inference costs, while retaining competitive
performance. SmolVLA is designed to be trained on a single GPU and deployed on
consumer-grade GPUs or even CPUs. To further improve responsiveness, we
introduce an asynchronous inference stack decoupling perception and action
prediction from action execution, allowing higher control rates with chunked
action generation. Despite its compact size, SmolVLA achieves performance
comparable to VLAs that are 10x larger. We evaluate SmolVLA on a range of both
simulated as well as real-world robotic benchmarks and release all code,
pretrained models, and training data.

keyInsights: Interleaving cross-attention and causal self-attention layers in the action expert yields superior performance (85.5% vs 79.0% for cross-attention only) by enabling both VLM feature conditioning and temporal action dependencies

Asynchronous inference with action chunk pre-fetching prevents robot idling and enables higher control rates while maintaining task success rates through observation similarity filtering and queue threshold management
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action models require billions of parameters and extensive computational resources, making them inaccessible to smaller research groups and preventing deployment on affordable robotic platforms
State-of-the-art VLA models rely on proprietary or large-scale academic datasets, limiting reproducibility and community participation in robotics research
results: Outperforms larger VLA models on LIBERO and Meta-World benchmarks while using significantly fewer resources, with 78.3% average success rate on real-world SO-100 manipulation tasks versus 48.3% for ACT and 61.7% for π0

Achieves 30% faster task completion through asynchronous inference (completing 19 pick-and-place cycles in 60 seconds versus 9 for synchronous mode) while maintaining comparable success rates and improving adaptability to environmental changes
solution: Develop a compact VLA architecture using SmolVLM-2 backbone with layer skipping (using only first N=L/2 layers) and visual token reduction to 64 per frame, combined with a Transformer-based action expert trained via flow matching
Train exclusively on community-driven datasets from affordable robotic platforms using automated VLM-based task annotation and camera viewpoint normalization to handle data heterogeneity
paper_summary.summary: Hugging Face researchers develop SmolVLA, a 450-million parameter vision-language-action model that achieves competitive robotic manipulation performance using 7x less memory and 40% faster training than larger VLA models like π0 (3.3B parameters), demonstrating 78.3% success rate on real-world SO-100 tasks compared to 61.7% for π0 and introducing an asynchronous inference stack that enables 30% faster task completion (9.7s vs 13.75s) by decoupling action prediction from execution, while being trained exclusively on community-contributed datasets totaling fewer than 30,000 episodes and deployable on consumer-grade hardware including CPUs.

title: SmolVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Affordable and Efficient  Robotics

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models typically bridge the gap between perceptual and action spaces by pre-training a large-scale Vision-Language Model (VLM) on robotic data. While this approach greatly enhances performance, it also incurs significant training costs. In this paper, we investigate how to effectively bridge vision-language (VL) representations to action (A). We introduce VLA-Adapter, a novel paradigm designed to reduce the reliance of VLA models on large-scale VLMs and extensive pre-training. To this end, we first systematically analyze the effectiveness of various VL conditions and present key findings on which conditions are essential for bridging perception and action spaces. Based on these insights, we propose a lightweight Policy module with Bridge Attention, which autonomously injects the optimal condition into the action space. In this way, our method achieves high performance using only a 0.5B-parameter backbone, without any robotic data pre-training. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world robotic benchmarks demonstrate that VLA-Adapter not only achieves state-of-the-art level performance, but also offers the fast inference speed reported to date. Furthermore, thanks to the proposed advanced bridging paradigm, VLA-Adapter enables the training of a powerful VLA model in just 8 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU, greatly lowering the barrier to deploying the VLA model. Project page: this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
State Key Laboratory of Networking and Switching Technology

keyInsights: Middle-layer raw latent features from the VLM are more effective for action generation than deep-layer ones, as they better balance multimodal integration with rich detail.
Deep-layer ActionQuery latents prove to be more effective, aggregating richer multimodal details from scratch for action prediction.

A learnable injection ratio for raw features in conjunction with direct use of ActionQuery features allows the Policy to effectively absorb and utilize essential multimodal information, enhancing performance and stability.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high training and inference costs, relying on large, multi-billion parameter Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
Extensive pre-training on robotic datasets is often required, leading to slow fine-tuning and high GPU memory consumption.
These factors limit the accessibility, real-time control capabilities, and deployment of VLA models on resource-constrained hardware.
results: VLA-Adapter achieved a 97.3% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark with a 0.5B parameter VLM backbone, matching or exceeding the performance of many 7B+ parameter models.
It demonstrated superior efficiency, achieving an inference throughput of 219.2 Hz (approximately 3 times faster than OpenVLA-OFT) and the fastest reported latency of 0.0365 seconds.
The model exhibited strong zero-shot generalization on the CALVIN ABC→D benchmark with a 4.42 average task length and successfully performed complex tasks in real-world robotic experiments.
solution: VLA-Adapter introduces a lightweight Policy network that efficiently bridges vision-language (VL) representations to action (A) by optimizing the multimodal conditions it receives from the VLM.
It systematically analyzes and integrates both all-layer raw VLM features and all-layer ActionQuery features using a novel Bridge Attention module within the Policy network.
A learnable injection ratio is incorporated into the Bridge Attention to selectively inject relevant information from raw features, ensuring optimal information transfer to the action space.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Westlake University, and Zhejiang University, along with the OpenHelix Team, introduce VLA-Adapter, an efficient method to bridge vision-language representations to robotic actions. The approach enables state-of-the-art level performance with a tiny-scale 0.5B parameter backbone without robotic data pre-training, achieving a 97.3% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark and providing a 3x faster inference speed (219.2 Hz) than comparable methods.

title: VLA-Adapter: An Effective Paradigm for Tiny-Scale Vision-Language-Action Model

abstract: Recent large-scale Vision Language Action (VLA) models have shown superior performance in robotic manipulation tasks guided by natural language. However, current VLA models suffer from two drawbacks: (i) generation of massive tokens leading to high inference latency and increased training cost, and (ii) insufficient utilization of generated actions resulting in potential performance loss. To address these issues, we develop a training framework to finetune VLA models for generating significantly fewer action tokens with high parallelism, effectively reducing inference latency and training cost. Furthermore, we introduce an inference optimization technique with a novel voting-based ensemble strategy to combine current and previous action predictions, improving the utilization of generated actions and overall performance. Our results demonstrate that we achieve superior performance compared with state-of-the-art VLA models, achieving significantly higher success rates and 39$\times$ faster inference than OpenVLA with 46 Hz throughput on edge platforms, demonstrating practical deployability. The code is available at this https URL.
title: VOTE: Vision-Language-Action Optimization with Trajectory Ensemble Voting

abstract:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown remarkable potential in
visuomotor control and instruction comprehension through end-to-end learning
processes. However, current VLA models face significant challenges: they are
slow during inference and require extensive pre-training on large amounts of
robotic data, making real-world deployment difficult. In this paper, we
introduce a new family of compact vision-language-action models, called
TinyVLA, which offers two key advantages over existing VLA models: (1) faster
inference speeds, and (2) improved data efficiency, eliminating the need for
pre-training stage. Our framework incorporates two essential components to
build TinyVLA: (1) initializing the policy backbone with robust, high-speed
multimodal models, and (2) integrating a diffusion policy decoder during
fine-tuning to enable precise robot actions. We conducted extensive evaluations
of TinyVLA in both simulation and on real robots, demonstrating that our
approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art VLA model, OpenVLA, in
terms of speed and data efficiency, while delivering comparable or superior
performance. Additionally, TinyVLA exhibits strong generalization capabilities
across various dimensions, including language instructions, novel objects,
unseen positions, changes in object appearance, background variations, and
environmental shifts, often matching or exceeding the performance of OpenVLA.
We believe that \methodname offers an interesting perspective on utilizing
pre-trained multimodal models for policy learning. Our project is at
this https URL

keyInsights: Robust Vision-Language-Action (VLA) capabilities can be achieved without extensive pre-training on massive robot-specific datasets, by effectively leveraging knowledge from general vision-language data.

The combination of compact, pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and a diffusion policy decoder provides a method for achieving both high inference speed and data efficiency in robotic manipulation.
VLM size scales with performance in TinyVLA, with larger VLMs (e.g., TinyVLA-H) mitigating issues such as instruction misinterpretation and inaccurate positioning.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, such as RT-2 and OpenVLA, suffer from slow inference speeds due to their reliance on large Vision-Language Models (often 7+ billion parameters) and autoregressive action token generation.
These VLA models require extensive pre-training on massive robotic datasets (e.g., 970K samples for OpenVLA), which is computationally expensive and resource-intensive.
Current VLA approaches face challenges in real-time, responsive robotic control and accessibility for researchers or applications with limited computational resources or data collection capabilities.
results: TinyVLA-H achieved a 94.0% average success rate in real-world single-arm manipulation tasks, outperforming OpenVLA's 68.3%. It also achieved 44.5% in bimanual tasks where OpenVLA failed (0%).

TinyVLA operates 20 times faster than OpenVLA (14 ms vs. 292 ms per action prediction) while using 5.5 times fewer parameters, and eliminates the need for extensive pre-training on large robotic datasets like OpenX.
The model demonstrated robust generalization capabilities across various scenarios, including novel instructions, camera viewpoints, backgrounds, illumination, distractors, and spatial variations.
solution: Utilizes compact, pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) ranging from 70 million to 1.4 billion parameters as the policy backbone, pre-trained on general vision-language data.
Employs Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning on robotic manipulation data, freezing most of the VLM's pre-trained weights to preserve knowledge and enhance data efficiency.
Integrates a Diffusion Policy (DP) as the policy head for direct, continuous robot action generation, departing from slow autoregressive token prediction.
paper_summary.summary: TinyVLA introduces a Vision-Language-Action model that achieves robust robotic manipulation performance while being significantly faster and more data-efficient than prior VLA models. It demonstrates that strong VLA capabilities can emerge without extensive pre-training on massive robotic datasets, leveraging compact pre-trained VLMs and a diffusion policy for action generation.

title: TinyVLA: Towards Fast, Data-Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models for  Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong multi-modal reasoning capabilities, enabling direct action generation from visual perception and language instructions in an end-to-end manner. However, their substantial computational cost poses a challenge for real-time robotic control, where rapid decision-making is essential. This paper introduces VLA-Cache, a training-free inference acceleration method that reduces computational overhead by adaptively caching and reusing static visual tokens across frames. Exploiting the temporal continuity in robotic manipulation, VLA-Cache identifies minimally changed tokens between adjacent frames and reuses their cached key-value representations, thereby circumventing redundant computations. Additionally, to maintain action precision, VLA-Cache selectively re-computes task-relevant tokens that are environmentally sensitive, ensuring the fidelity of critical visual information. To further optimize efficiency, we introduce a layer adaptive token reusing strategy that dynamically adjusts the reuse ratio based on attention concentration across decoder layers, prioritizing critical tokens for recomputation. Extensive experiments on two simulation platforms (LIBERO and SIMPLER) and a real-world robotic system demonstrate that VLA-Cache achieves up to 1.7x speedup in CUDA latency and a 15% increase in control frequency, with negligible loss on task success rate. The code and videos can be found at our project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Temporal continuity and spatial redundancy in sequential visual observations within robotic tasks offer significant opportunities for cross-frame computational reuse.
Distinguishing between visually static but task-relevant tokens and genuinely irrelevant static tokens is crucial; naive reuse of all static tokens degrades performance.
Cross-attention scores from the VLA model's language decoder can effectively and efficiently serve as a proxy for identifying task-relevant visual regions.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art VLA models have high computational costs, making real-time deployment in time-critical robotic control applications challenging.
Existing VLA models redundantly recompute all visual tokens at every timestep, even for static scene elements, leading to inefficiencies.
Prior acceleration methods often require architectural modifications, extensive retraining, or degrade spatial fidelity crucial for precise robotic manipulation.
results: Achieved up to 1.63x speedup in CUDA latency and a 27.31% reduction in FLOPs for OpenVLA, increasing control frequency from 4.23 Hz to 4.59 Hz with only a 0.3% average drop in success rate.
Demonstrated additive gains when applied to high-frequency models, boosting OpenVLA-OFT's control frequency from 65.10 Hz to 78.98 Hz.

Improved the average success rate by 2.4% (from 82.1% to 84.6%) on a real Kinova Jaco2 robot across four manipulation tasks and maintained baseline performance under dynamic backgrounds while reducing FLOPs by 42%.
solution: VLA-Cache implements a training-free inference acceleration method that reuses Key-Value (KV) representations of visually static tokens across consecutive frames.
It identifies static tokens based on patch-level cosine similarity and filters out 'task-relevant' static tokens by analyzing cross-attention scores from the language decoder.
A layer-adaptive token reusing strategy, guided by attention entropy, dynamically adjusts the reuse proportion across different decoder layers to further optimize efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-Cache introduces an adaptive token caching framework that reduces the computational overhead of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by intelligently reusing static and non-task-relevant visual tokens across consecutive frames. The method achieves up to 1.63x speedup in CUDA latency and a 27.31% FLOP reduction in simulations with minimal performance drop, and improves real-world robot success rates by 2.4%.

title: VLA-Cache: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation via Adaptive Token Caching

abstract: In this report, we introduce Xiaomi-Robotics-0, an advanced vision-language-action (VLA) model optimized for high performance and fast and smooth real-time execution. The key to our method lies in a carefully designed training recipe and deployment strategy. Xiaomi-Robotics-0 is first pre-trained on large-scale cross-embodiment robot trajectories and vision-language data, endowing it with broad and generalizable action-generation capabilities while avoiding catastrophic forgetting of the visual-semantic knowledge of the underlying pre-trained VLM. During post-training, we propose several techniques for training the VLA model for asynchronous execution to address the inference latency during real-robot rollouts. During deployment, we carefully align the timesteps of consecutive predicted action chunks to ensure continuous and seamless real-time rollouts. We evaluate Xiaomi-Robotics-0 extensively in simulation benchmarks and on two challenging real-robot tasks that require precise and dexterous bimanual manipulation. Results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across all simulation benchmarks. Moreover, Xiaomi-Robotics-0 can roll out fast and smoothly on real robots using a consumer-grade GPU, achieving high success rates and throughput on both real-robot tasks. To facilitate future research, code and model checkpoints are open-sourced at this https URL

keyInsights: Real-time VLA execution on consumer-grade GPUs (80 ms inference latency) is achievable for large models through optimized asynchronous strategies, minimizing idle time and jerky movements.

Specific attention mechanisms, like the Λ-shape attention mask, are crucial for balancing smooth action chaining with policy reactivity in asynchronous execution by forcing later predictions to attend to visual and linguistic cues.
Co-training VLA models on extensive robot trajectory data and curated vision-language data effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting of VLM capabilities and can even enhance embodied reasoning.
originalProblem: High inference latency of large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, often in the billions of parameters, causes pauses or jerky motions in real-robot deployments.
Existing asynchronous execution methods for VLA models can lead to policies that over-rely on action prefixes, diminishing reactivity to visual observations and language instructions.
Preventing catastrophic forgetting of general vision-language knowledge while endowing VLA models with action-generation capabilities is a significant challenge.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 98.7% on the LIBERO benchmark and improved average task completion length on CALVIN, demonstrating competitive performance in simulation.

Attained the highest throughput in real-robot bimanual Lego Disassembly and Towel Folding tasks, with 1.2 pieces per minute for towel folding, by mitigating repetitive loops observed in other asynchronous methods.
Outperformed comparable VLA baselines across most vision-language benchmarks, largely preserving the underlying VLM's performance and slightly surpassing Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct on the ERQA benchmark.
solution: Employs a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, integrating a pre-trained VLM (Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct) with a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for T-step action chunk generation via flow-matching.

Utilizes a two-stage training process: pre-training for VLM action generation and VL preservation, followed by post-training that adapts the model for real-robot synchronous or asynchronous execution.

Introduces a Λ-shape attention mask and positional encoding offset during asynchronous post-training to enhance policy reactivity by preventing over-reliance on action prefixes, alongside dynamic loss reweighting.
paper_summary.summary: Xiaomi-Robotics-0 is an open-sourced vision-language-action model from Xiaomi Robotics that achieves high performance and real-time execution, demonstrating competitive success rates across simulation benchmarks and improved throughput in real-robot bimanual manipulation tasks while preserving vision-language capabilities. The model's optimized asynchronous execution enables smooth, continuous robot operation with an 80 ms inference latency on consumer-grade GPUs.

title: Xiaomi-Robotics-0: An Open-Sourced Vision-Language-Action Model with Real-Time Execution

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for general-purpose robotic manipulation, leveraging large-scale pre-training to achieve strong performance. The field has rapidly evolved with additional spatial priors and diverse architectural innovations. However, these advancements are often accompanied by varying training recipes and implementation details, which can make it challenging to disentangle the precise source of empirical gains. In this work, we introduce SimVLA, a streamlined baseline designed to establish a transparent reference point for VLA research. By strictly decoupling perception from control, using a standard vision-language backbone and a lightweight action head, and standardizing critical training dynamics, we demonstrate that a minimal design can achieve state-of-the-art performance. Despite having only 0.5B parameters, SimVLA outperforms multi-billion-parameter models on standard simulation benchmarks without robot pretraining. SimVLA also reaches on-par real-robot performance compared to pi0.5. Our results establish SimVLA as a robust, reproducible baseline that enables clear attribution of empirical gains to future architectural innovations. Website: this https URL

keyInsights: Meticulous standardization of training and inference details, referred to as "silent drivers," is more impactful on VLA model performance than architectural complexity alone.
A minimalist architectural design, when rigorously trained, can achieve state-of-the-art performance in robotic manipulation, effectively challenging the necessity of multi-billion-parameter models.
Decoupling the vision-language backbone from the action head creates a modular and adaptable system, allowing for easy integration of future Vision-Language Model (VLM) advancements.
originalProblem: Performance attribution in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models is obscured by increasing architectural complexity, varied pretraining, and diverse optimization schedules.
The lack of standardized training practices impedes reproducibility and fair comparisons across different VLA models.
There is a prevalent assumption that advanced robotic manipulation capabilities necessitate ever-increasing architectural complexity in VLA designs.
results: SimVLA achieves an average 98.6% success rate on the LIBERO benchmark, surpassing multi-billion-parameter models like OpenVLA-OFT (97.1%) and MemoryVLA (96.7%) with a 0.5B parameter backbone.
It exhibits strong zero-shot generalization on LIBERO-PRO, achieving superior semantic and task robustness, with a 10% improvement over baselines on Goal and Long suites.

The model demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on WidowX robot tasks (95.8% average success) and competitive results on Google Robot tasks (76.1% average success), showcasing efficient real-robot transfer capabilities.
solution: SimVLA adopts a modular architecture, strictly decoupling a pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM) for perception from a lightweight Transformer-based action head for continuous control.
Continuous action generation is modeled using conditional flow matching, which learns a deterministic vector field to transform Gaussian noise into desired action chunks efficiently.
The approach meticulously standardizes training and inference recipes, including data handling, optimization dynamics, action representation, and VLM backbone fine-tuning strategies.
paper_summary.summary: A streamlined Vision-Language-Action (VLA) baseline, SimVLA, demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in robotic manipulation, achieving an average 98.6% success rate on the LIBERO benchmark with a compact 0.5B parameter model. This performance is attributed to a modular architecture and rigorous standardization of training details, outperforming larger, more complex VLA models.

title: SimVLA: A Simple VLA Baseline for Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful framework that unifies perception, language, and control, enabling robots to perform diverse tasks through multimodal understanding. However, current VLA models typically contain massive parameters and rely heavily on large-scale robot data pretraining, leading to high computational costs during training, as well as limited deployability for real-time inference. Moreover, most training paradigms often degrade the perceptual representations of the vision-language backbone, resulting in overfitting and poor generalization to downstream tasks. In this work, we present Evo-1, a lightweight VLA model that reduces computation and improves deployment efficiency, while maintaining strong performance without pretraining on robot data. Evo-1 builds on a native multimodal Vision-Language model (VLM), incorporating a novel cross-modulated diffusion transformer along with an optimized integration module, together forming an effective architecture. We further introduce a two-stage training paradigm that progressively aligns action with perception, preserving the representations of the VLM. Notably, with only 0.77 billion parameters, Evo-1 achieves state-of-the-art results on the Meta-World and RoboTwin suite, surpassing the previous best models by 12.4% and 6.9%, respectively, and also attains a competitive result of 94.8% on LIBERO. In real-world evaluations, Evo-1 attains a 78% success rate with high inference frequency and low memory overhead, outperforming all baseline methods. We release code, data, and model weights to facilitate future research on lightweight and efficient VLA models.

keyInsights: Employing a Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone natively pre-trained on multimodal data significantly enhances cross-modal alignment, which is crucial for visuomotor tasks.

A two-stage training paradigm, where the action expert is aligned with a frozen VLM before full fine-tuning, is effective in preserving the VLM's semantic representations, leading to improved generalization and reduced overfitting.

A cross-attention-based integration module, specifically using concatenated multimodal and proprioceptive features as key-value inputs to the action expert's mid-layers, optimizes information flow for robust action generation.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are prohibitively large, demanding extensive computational resources and GPU memory, which hinders real-time deployment on robotic hardware.
High computational overhead leads to low control frequencies, limiting real-time responsiveness critical for dynamic and interactive robotic tasks.
End-to-end fine-tuning of pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for action generation often degrades their inherent semantic and perceptual representations, leading to task-specific overfitting.
Existing high-performing VLA models often rely on costly and labor-intensive large-scale robot datasets for pretraining, limiting accessibility and scalability.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across simulation benchmarks, including 80.6% on Meta-World, 94.8% on LIBERO, and 37.8% on RoboTwin (dual-arm), surpassing previous larger models.
Demonstrated an average 78% success rate in diverse real-world tasks with an xArm6 robot, significantly outperforming baselines while being roughly one-fourth the size of comparable models.
Exhibited high inference efficiency, using only 2.3 GB of GPU memory and achieving an inference frequency of 16.4 Hz, making it suitable for real-time deployment on consumer-grade hardware.

Showed superior generalization capabilities in real-world environments under various unseen disturbances (e.g., distractors, background changes, position variations), with ablation studies confirming the benefits of the two-stage training paradigm.
solution: A modular Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architecture leveraging a lightweight InternVL3-1B backbone (0.77B parameters total) for efficient multimodal encoding and a Cross-modulated Diffusion Transformer (action expert) for continuous action generation.
The action expert employs a flow-matching paradigm with a unique Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that uses only stacked cross-attention layers, conditioned on VLM representations and robot state.

A two-stage training procedure is implemented: first, freeze the VLM backbone to align the action expert, then fine-tune the entire architecture to preserve semantic alignment and enhance task adaptation.
paper_summary.summary: Evo-1 introduces a lightweight Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that achieves state-of-the-art robotic manipulation performance with significantly reduced computational requirements. The model, comprising 0.77 billion parameters, utilizes only 2.3 GB of GPU memory and reaches an inference frequency of 16.4 Hz, outperforming larger VLA systems across diverse simulation and real-world benchmarks without extensive robot data pretraining.

title: Evo-1: Lightweight Vision-Language-Action Model with Preserved Semantic Alignment

abstract: A fundamental objective in robot manipulation is to enable models to comprehend visual scenes and execute actions. Although existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robots can handle a range of basic tasks, they still face challenges in two areas: (1) insufficient reasoning ability to tackle complex tasks, and (2) high computational costs for VLA model fine-tuning and inference. The recently proposed state space model (SSM) known as Mamba demonstrates promising capabilities in non-trivial sequence modeling with linear inference complexity. Inspired by this, we introduce RoboMamba, an end-to-end robotic VLA model that leverages Mamba to deliver both robotic reasoning and action capabilities, while maintaining efficient fine-tuning and inference. Specifically, we first integrate the vision encoder with Mamba, aligning visual tokens with language embedding through co-training, empowering our model with visual common sense and robotic-related reasoning. To further equip RoboMamba with SE(3) pose prediction abilities, we explore an efficient fine-tuning strategy with a simple policy head. We find that once RoboMamba possesses sufficient reasoning capability, it can acquire manipulation skills with minimal fine-tuning parameters (0.1\% of the model) and time. In experiments, RoboMamba demonstrates outstanding reasoning capabilities on general and robotic evaluation benchmarks. Meanwhile, our model showcases impressive pose prediction results in both simulation and real-world experiments, achieving inference speeds 3 times faster than existing VLA models. Our project web page: this https URL

keyInsights: Mamba's linear complexity and strong context-aware reasoning make it an effective and efficient architectural backbone for multimodal robotic VLA models, overcoming Transformer's computational limitations.
Developing strong, dedicated robotic-related reasoning capabilities in an MLLM is foundational and directly correlates with improved manipulation accuracy and generalization.

Once an MLLM possesses sufficient reasoning abilities, the acquisition of specific low-level manipulation skills can be achieved with extremely minimal fine-tuning (e.g., updating only ~0.1% of parameters in minutes).
originalProblem: Existing MLLM-based robotic VLA models often exhibit insufficient reasoning capabilities for complex robotic scenarios, such as long-horizon planning or affordance understanding.
Traditional Transformer-based MLLMs incur high computational costs due to quadratic attention complexity, making them impractical for real-time robotic control and expensive to fine-tune.
Acquiring low-level robotic manipulation skills typically requires extensive fine-tuning of a large proportion of the MLLM's parameters, demanding significant computational resources.
results: RoboMamba achieves state-of-the-art manipulation success rates in simulation, improving 'seen' tasks by 7.0% and 'unseen' tasks by 2.0% over the previous best methods.

It demonstrates significantly faster inference, up to 7 times quicker than LLaMA-AdapterV2 and ManipLLM, and acquires manipulation skills by fine-tuning only 0.1% (3.7M) of its parameters in dozens of minutes.

The model exhibits superior robotic-related reasoning on the RoboVQA dataset and competitive performance on general VQA benchmarks, while also showing improved hallucination reduction on the POPE benchmark.
solution: RoboMamba integrates a pre-trained CLIP vision encoder with a Mamba (State Space Model) Large Language Model and a small, dedicated MLP policy head for 6-DoF end-effector pose prediction.

A two-stage training pipeline is employed: first, cross-modal alignment and instruction co-training for general and robotic-related reasoning (freezing CLIP encoder, fine-tuning Mamba LLM); second, efficient manipulation fine-tuning.

During manipulation fine-tuning, all parameters of the pre-trained RoboMamba (vision encoder, Mamba LLM) are frozen, with only the simple, 0.1% parameter policy head being updated to learn action skills.
paper_summary.summary: RoboMamba introduces an efficient Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model for robotic reasoning and manipulation by leveraging the Mamba State Space Model. It achieves up to 7 times faster inference speeds and acquires manipulation skills by fine-tuning only 0.1% of its parameters, while improving manipulation success rates by up to 7.0% on 'seen' tasks in simulation.

title: RoboMamba: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model for Robotic Reasoning and Manipulation

abstract: Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models built on pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) require extensive post-training, resulting in high computational overhead that limits scalability and this http URL propose CogVLA, a Cognition-Aligned Vision-Language-Action framework that leverages instruction-driven routing and sparsification to improve both efficiency and performance. CogVLA draws inspiration from human multimodal coordination and introduces a 3-stage progressive architecture. 1) Encoder-FiLM based Aggregation Routing (EFA-Routing) injects instruction information into the vision encoder to selectively aggregate and compress dual-stream visual tokens, forming a instruction-aware latent representation. 2) Building upon this compact visual encoding, LLM-FiLM based Pruning Routing (LFP-Routing) introduces action intent into the language model by pruning instruction-irrelevant visually grounded tokens, thereby achieving token-level sparsity. 3) To ensure that compressed perception inputs can still support accurate and coherent action generation, we introduce V-L-A Coupled Attention (CAtten), which combines causal vision-language attention with bidirectional action parallel decoding. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate that CogVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance with success rates of 97.4% and 70.0%, respectively, while reducing training costs by 2.5-fold and decreasing inference latency by 2.8-fold compared to OpenVLA. CogVLA is open-sourced and publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Emulating human cognitive mechanisms, such as selective attention and multimodal coordination, provides a viable architectural blueprint for designing efficient and performant VLA models.
Instruction-driven dynamic routing and sparsification across different modalities can drastically reduce computational overhead without compromising crucial task-relevant semantic information.

Achieving a synergistic balance between early-stage aggressive visual compression and later-stage context-aware pruning, combined with parallel action decoding, is key to enhancing both VLA model efficiency and performance.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face prohibitive computational costs during training and inference, leading to high memory usage, FLOPs, and prolonged training times that limit scalability.

Existing sparsification and acceleration techniques for VLA models often focus on isolated modules, causing 'cross-modal semantic degradation' where task-relevant fine-grained features are lost and contextual coherence is disrupted.
A lack of holistic frameworks that effectively balance computational efficiency with the maintenance of semantic consistency across vision, language, and action modalities.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance with a 97.4% average task success rate on the LIBERO simulation benchmark, outperforming OpenVLA (76.5%) and other advanced VLA models.
Demonstrated a 70.0% overall success rate on complex real-world ALOHA manipulation tasks, surpassing existing methods like STAR (45.0%) and OpenVLA-OFT (56.7%).

Realized substantial efficiency gains, including 2.79x faster inference time (0.091s), 22.54x higher throughput (87.9 Hz), 3.12x fewer FLOPs (2.72 TFLOPs), and a 2.49x reduction in training cost compared to OpenVLA.
solution: A 3-stage progressive architecture (EFA-Routing, LFP-Routing, CAtten) inspired by human cognitive processes (Visual Attention System, Supplementary Motor Area, Premotor Cortex) for multimodal coordination.

Instruction-driven Encoder-FiLM based Aggregation Routing (EFA-Routing) and LLM-FiLM based Pruning Routing (LFP-Routing) dynamically sparsify visual and language tokens, reducing up to 8x visual input redundancy.

V-L-A Coupled Attention (CAtten) with a hierarchical hybrid attention mask enables parallel decoding of action chunks, ensuring semantic consistency and temporal coherence for efficient action generation.
paper_summary.summary: CogVLA, developed by researchers at Harbin Institute of Technology, introduces a cognition-aligned, instruction-driven framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. It achieves a 97.4% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark and 70.0% on real-world ALOHA tasks, while also reducing inference time by 2.79x and FLOPs by 3.12x compared to leading baselines.

title: CogVLA: Cognition-Aligned Vision-Language-Action Model via Instruction-Driven Routing & Sparsification

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pre-trained on large, diverse datasets show remarkable potential for general-purpose robotic manipulation. However, a primary bottleneck remains in adapting these models to downstream tasks, especially when the robot's embodiment or the task itself differs from the pre-training data. This discrepancy leads to a significant mismatch in action distributions, demanding extensive data and compute for effective fine-tuning. To address this challenge, we introduce \textbf{Align-Then-stEer (\texttt{ATE})}, a novel, data-efficient, and plug-and-play adaptation framework. \texttt{ATE} first aligns disparate action spaces by constructing a unified latent space, where a variational autoencoder constrained by reverse KL divergence embeds adaptation actions into modes of the pre-training action latent distribution. Subsequently, it steers the diffusion- or flow-based VLA's generation process during fine-tuning via a guidance mechanism that pushes the model's output distribution towards the target domain. We conduct extensive experiments on cross-embodiment and cross-task manipulation in both simulation and real world. Compared to direct fine-tuning of representative VLAs, our method improves the average multi-task success rate by up to \textbf{9.8\%} in simulation and achieves a striking \textbf{32\% success rate gain} in a real-world cross-embodiment setting. Our work presents a general and lightweight solution that greatly enhances the practicality of deploying VLA models to new robotic platforms and tasks.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
Institute of Artificial Intelligence, China Telecom

keyInsights: Bridging action distribution discrepancies through a hierarchically structured, unified latent space is crucial for data-efficient cross-embodiment and cross-task VLA adaptation.

Employing reverse KL divergence during latent space construction effectively embeds adaptation actions into the modes of broader pre-training distributions, leveraging prior knowledge and preventing distribution collapse.
Classifier guidance, when applied within this unified latent space, provides an efficient mechanism to steer VLA policy generation towards desired behaviors using minimal adaptation data.
originalProblem: Pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a bottleneck in efficiently adapting to new robotic embodiments or distinct downstream tasks.
A substantial "action distribution mismatch" occurs between pre-training and adaptation data due to varying robot kinematics, degrees of freedom, and task-specific action requirements.
Current adaptation techniques often demand extensive and costly data collection and fine-tuning, limiting the practical scalability and deployment of VLA models.
results: ATE achieved an average multi-task success rate gain of +9.8% for RDT-1B and +8.7% for π0 in simulation, and a 32% success rate gain on a real-world dual-arm robot compared to direct fine-tuning baselines.

The framework demonstrated enhanced generalization across various real-world perturbations including varying illumination, spatial shifts, visual distractors, and human disturbances, exhibiting increased robustness.

Ablation studies confirmed that the two-stage Info-VAE training scheme, which aligns adaptation actions into pre-trained latent manifold modes, is critical for achieving higher final success rates and efficient adaptation.
solution: The "Align" stage constructs a unified latent space using two InfoVAEs: one for pre-training actions and a second for adaptation actions, embedding the latter into modes of the former via reverse KL divergence.

The "Steer" stage integrates classifier guidance, derived from this unified latent space, to explicitly direct the generative process of diffusion- or flow-based VLAs during fine-tuning towards the target action distribution.

The framework is designed as a model-agnostic, plug-and-play solution that integrates with various diffusion/flow-based VLAs without architectural modifications and incurs negligible computational overhead.
paper_summary.summary: A framework called "Align-Then-stEer (ATE)" enables pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to adapt to new robotic embodiments and diverse tasks using limited data. This is achieved through a two-stage process that establishes a unified latent space for action representation and then steers the generative VLA policies, yielding notable success rate improvements and enhanced robustness in both simulated and real-world robotic manipulation.

title: Align-Then-stEer: Adapting the Vision-Language Action Models through Unified Latent Guidance

abstract: The advancement of large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models has significantly improved robotic manipulation in terms of language-guided task execution and generalization to unseen scenarios. While existing VLAs adapted from pretrained large Vision-Language-Models (VLM) have demonstrated promising generalizability, their task performance is still unsatisfactory as indicated by the low tasks success rates in different environments. In this paper, we present a new advanced VLA architecture derived from VLM. Unlike previous works that directly repurpose VLM for action prediction by simple action quantization, we propose a omponentized VLA architecture that has a specialized action module conditioned on VLM output. We systematically study the design of the action module and demonstrates the strong performance enhancement with diffusion action transformers for action sequence modeling, as well as their favorable scaling behaviors. We also conduct comprehensive experiments and ablation studies to evaluate the efficacy of our models with varied designs. The evaluation on 5 robot embodiments in simulation and real work shows that our model not only significantly surpasses existing VLAs in task performance and but also exhibits remarkable adaptation to new robots and generalization to unseen objects and backgrounds. It exceeds the average success rates of OpenVLA which has similar model size (7B) with ours by over 35% in simulated evaluation and 55% in real robot experiments. It also outperforms the large RT-2-X model (55B) by 18% absolute success rates in simulation. Code and models can be found on our project page (this https URL).

keyInsights: Treating robotic action generation as distinct from language generation, requiring specialized modeling approaches for continuous, multimodal, and temporally correlated actions.

A componentized architecture where a dedicated diffusion transformer action module, conditioned by cognitive features from a VLM, effectively models complex action distributions without discretization.
Adaptive action ensemble during inference can smooth action trajectories and improve robustness by combining multiple predictions based on similarity.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often quantize continuous action spaces into discrete bins, leading to imprecise robotic control.
These models struggle to capture the inherent temporal correlation between successive robot actions and represent multimodal action distributions (multiple valid ways to complete a task).
VLA models often face challenges in generalizing to new robots, environments, and objects in real-world scenarios.
results: CogACT achieved a 61.3% success rate on variant aggregation tasks and 74.8% on visual matching tasks in SIMPLER simulation with Google Robot, outperforming RT-2-X (54.4% and 46.3%).

In real-world tests, CogACT demonstrated superior performance, including a 71.2% success rate on seen environments and 61.5% on unseen environments with the Realman robot, and 61.4% with unseen objects on the Franka robot.
The research showed that scaling up the specialized action module, even modestly, leads to significant performance improvements, with the large action module achieving a 65.7% average success rate.
solution: CogACT introduces a componentized Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architecture that separates task understanding (cognition) from execution (action) through distinct vision, language, and action modules.

It employs a specialized action module based on a diffusion transformer (DiT) that directly models continuous and multimodal robotic action distributions, conditioned by cognitive features from the VLM.
The model integrates an adaptive action ensemble algorithm during inference to generate and combine multiple action predictions, enhancing robustness and smoothing action trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: CogACT is a Vision-Language-Action model that improves robotic manipulation by adopting a componentized architecture and a specialized diffusion-based action module. This approach directly models continuous and multimodal robotic actions, leading to significantly higher success rates across diverse simulation and real-world tasks compared to existing VLA models.

title: CogACT: A Foundational Vision-Language-Action Model for Synergizing Cognition and Action in Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Deploying powerful Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on edge devices is limited by their massive size. In this paper, we take a deployment-oriented view of VLA training: we target efficiency through model design and optimization, rather than relying solely on post-hoc compression. Thus, we propose BitVLA, a fully native 1-bit VLA model for robotic manipulation, where every parameters is ternary, i.e., {-1,0,1}. BitVLA is built on the publicly available 1-bit LLM BitNet b1.58 2B4T, and is trained as a vision-language-action policy that inherits the compactness of 1-bit pretraining while retaining strong task performance. To further reduce the memory footprint of the vision backbone, we introduce Quantize-then-Distill, a post-training quantization-aware training strategy that compresses a full-precision vision encoder to 1.58-bit weights, while a full-precision teacher guides representation alignment during training. Across simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks, BitVLA matches the performance of the full-precision OpenVLA-OFT baseline, while reducing model memory by 11.0x and end-to-end latency by 4.4x. These results suggest a practical path toward training-time efficiency-accuracy co-design for embodied policies, enabling competitive manipulation capability on memory-constrained edge robotic platforms. We release the code in this https URL, model weights in this https URL.

keyInsights: Aggressive 1-bit quantization can be successfully integrated into VLA models for robotic manipulation with competitive performance, provided a co-designed, training-time approach is used.

The 'Quantize-then-Distill' strategy effectively compresses the vision encoder to 1.58-bit while maintaining critical multimodal representation alignment, outperforming post-training quantization for VLA applications.
Large-scale robotics pre-training is crucial for BitVLA to acquire generalizable manipulation knowledge and achieve robust performance on diverse simulation and real-world tasks.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are large and rely on full-precision parameters, resulting in high memory footprints and significant inference latency, making them impractical for resource-constrained edge robotics.
Post-training quantization (PTQ) for VLA models often leads to accuracy degradation and complex calibration, without being inherently aligned with the model's training dynamics.

The potential of extreme low-bit models (like 1-bit LLMs) for multimodal VLA models remains largely unexplored, due to concerns about quantization-induced representation mismatches affecting vision-language alignment and action prediction.
results: BitVLA achieved an average success rate of 96.0% on the LIBERO benchmark, comparable to OpenVLA-OFT (97.1%) while reducing memory footprint by 11.0x (1.4 GB vs. 15.4 GB).
The model exhibited 4.4x lower inference latency (73 ms) and 4.4x higher throughput (341.1 Hz) compared to OpenVLA-OFT+, enabling deployment on consumer-grade GPUs.
BitVLA demonstrated robust performance in real-world manipulation tasks and generalized effectively to out-of-distribution scenarios and novel objects in a zero-shot manner.
solution: BitVLA was developed as a fully native 1-bit VLA model, constraining all parameters in the LLM backbone and vision encoder to ternary values ({-1, 0, 1}) for weights and INT8 for activations.

A three-stage training paradigm was employed: multimodal training for stable vision-language initialization, a 'Quantize-then-Distill' stage to compress the vision encoder, and a robotics training stage for manipulation capabilities.

The 'Quantize-then-Distill' stage uses a full-precision teacher to guide the quantization of a 1.58-bit student vision encoder, incorporating an auxiliary representation alignment loss to mitigate quantization-induced drift and preserve multimodal alignment.
paper_summary.summary: BitVLA introduces the first 1-bit Vision-Language-Action model for robotic manipulation, significantly reducing memory footprint and inference latency. It achieves comparable performance to larger full-precision VLA models on robotic tasks, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

title: BitVLA: 1-bit Vision-Language-Action Models for Robotics Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language Action (VLA) models significantly advance robotic manipulation by leveraging the strong perception capabilities of pretrained vision-language models (VLMs). By integrating action modules into these pretrained models, VLA methods exhibit improved generalization. However, training them from scratch is costly. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective distillation-based framework that equips VLMs with action-execution capability by transferring knowledge from pretrained small action models. Our architecture retains the original VLM structure, adding only an action token and a state encoder to incorporate physical inputs. To distill action knowledge, we adopt a two-stage training strategy. First, we perform lightweight alignment by mapping VLM hidden states into the action space of the small action model, enabling effective reuse of its pretrained action decoder and avoiding expensive pretraining. Second, we selectively fine-tune the language model, state encoder, and action modules, enabling the system to integrate multimodal inputs with precise action generation. Specifically, the action token provides the VLM with a direct handle for predicting future actions, while the state encoder allows the model to incorporate robot dynamics not captured by vision alone. This design yields substantial efficiency gains over training large VLA models from scratch. Compared with previous state-of-the-art methods, our method achieves 97.3% average success rate on LIBERO (11.8% improvement) and 93.5% on LIBERO-LONG (24.5% improvement). In real-world experiments across five manipulation tasks, our method consistently outperforms the teacher model, achieving 82.0% success rate (17% improvement), which demonstrate that action distillation effectively enables VLMs to generate precise actions while substantially reducing training costs.

keyInsights: Knowledge distillation from small, efficient action models can effectively equip large VLMs with superior action-execution capabilities, greatly reducing the need for costly end-to-end VLA pretraining.
Actively integrating robot state information and enabling the VLM to participate in action modeling via an action token is crucial for precise, generalized control.

A two-stage training strategy involving initial representation alignment followed by end-to-end fine-tuning consistently outperforms direct fine-tuning, validating the importance of representation space alignment.
originalProblem: Training Vision-Language Action (VLA) models end-to-end is computationally expensive and requires massive datasets.
Existing VLA architectures either omit crucial robot state information (discretization-based) or underutilize the VLM's full potential for end-to-end action modeling (diffusion-based).
Large VLA models often lag behind smaller, task-specific models in embodied benchmarks, indicating a gap in precise low-level action control.
results: Achieved a 92.5% first-task success rate on the CALVIN ABC-D benchmark (zero-shot generalization) and a 97.3% average success rate across all LIBERO tasks, marking an 11.8% improvement over previous state-of-the-art VLA methods.
Demonstrated an 82.0% average success rate on real-world ALOHA robot tasks, showing a 17.0% improvement over the teacher model, Seer, and superior performance in complex, long-horizon manipulations.
The two-stage training strategy boosted Task 1 success on CALVIN ABC-D by 6.5% and LIBERO-LONG average success by 1% compared to direct fine-tuning, empirically validating its effectiveness.
solution: Proposes a VLA architecture that extends the VITA-1.5-7B model by integrating a dedicated state encoder and a learnable action token for multimodal context processing.
Introduces a two-stage knowledge distillation framework to transfer robust action modeling abilities from a small, pretrained action expert (teacher) to the VLM (student).
Reuses a fixed, pretrained 2-layer MLP action decoder from an expert model, where the VLM's mapped hidden states are fed to generate 7-DoF executable actions.
paper_summary.summary: The VITA-VLA framework introduces an efficient, distillation-based approach to teach Vision-Language Models (VLMs) robotic action generation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on complex simulation benchmarks and real-world tasks with significantly reduced training costs.

title: VITA-VLA: Efficiently Teaching Vision-Language Models to Act via Action Expert Distillation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a compelling framework for tackling complex robotic manipulation tasks, but they are often expensive to train. In this paper, we propose a novel VLA approach that leverages the competitive performance of Vision Language Models (VLMs) on 2D images to directly infer robot end-effector poses in image frame coordinates. Unlike prior VLA models that output low-level controls, our model predicts trajectory waypoints, making it both more efficient to train and robot embodiment agnostic. Despite its lightweight design, our next-token prediction architecture effectively learns meaningful and executable robot trajectories. We further explore the underutilized potential of incorporating depth images, inference-time techniques such as decoding strategies, and demonstration-conditioned action generation. Our model is trained on a simulated dataset and exhibits strong sim-to-real transfer capabilities. We evaluate our approach using a combination of simulated and real data, demonstrating its effectiveness on a real robotic system.

keyInsights: Predicting end-effector keyposes in camera-frame coordinates, rather than robot-base coordinates, leverages VLM strengths and improves VLA performance, offering robot embodiment agnosticism.
Integrating depth information via colorized depth images significantly enhances VLA performance and reduces failure rates by providing crucial 3D spatial context.
Carefully randomized and diverse simulated datasets, especially with varied 3D assets, are crucial for achieving robust sim-to-real transfer and generalization to novel objects.
originalProblem: Large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face immense computational costs, making research and development difficult for many groups.
High-quality, multimodal real-world datasets for VLA training are prohibitively expensive and time-consuming to collect.
Standardized evaluation of VLA models relies on hard-to-reproduce real-world robot rollouts and lacks robust metrics for diverse predictions.
results: The cVLA system demonstrates strong zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, successfully performing 15 distinct tabletop manipulation tasks on a real Franka Panda robot without real-world fine-tuning.

The proposed `beam-search-NMS` decoding strategy significantly improves multi-trajectory prediction, reducing Top-3 error from 33.94% to 25.00% and achieving a substantially higher mAP (0.45 with crop vs. 0.16 for standard beam search).
cVLA successfully extends to one-shot imitation learning, achieving 70% success on CLEVR-easy and 28% on CLEVR-hard simulated tasks, showing generalization to real data and unseen objects.
solution: A lightweight VLA system built on a fine-tuned PaliGemma2 VLM that predicts two key trajectory waypoints directly in 2D image-frame coordinates for a single forward pass.
Training uses a meticulously curated simulated dataset from ManiSkill, incorporating diverse 3D assets (CLEVR, Objaverse) and strong randomization strategies for sim-to-real transfer.
Novel inference-time strategies are introduced, including input image cropping and `beam-search-NMS` for generating and evaluating diverse, spatially distinct trajectory predictions.
paper_summary.summary: The University of Freiburg's cVLA presents an efficient Vision-Language-Action (VLA) system that predicts end-effector keyposes directly in camera-space coordinates, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for tabletop manipulation tasks with a real robot arm. This system leverages fine-tuned pre-trained VLMs and a multi-level decoding strategy, achieving superior multi-trajectory prediction and successful execution on real-world objects.

title: cVLA: Towards Efficient Camera-Space VLAs

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong performance in robotic manipulation, yet their closed-loop deployment is hindered by the high latency and compute cost of repeatedly running large vision-language backbones at every timestep. We observe that VLA inference exhibits structured redundancies across temporal, spatial, and depth dimensions, and that most existing efficiency methods ignore action context, despite its central role in embodied tasks. To address this gap, we propose Action-Context-aware Adaptive Computation for VLA models (AC^2-VLA), a unified framework that conditions computation on current visual observations, language instructions, and previous action states. Based on this action-centric context, AC^2-VLA adaptively performs cognition reuse across timesteps, token pruning, and selective execution of model components within a unified mechanism. To train the adaptive policy, we introduce an action-guided self-distillation scheme that preserves the behavior of the dense VLA policy while enabling structured sparsification that transfers across tasks and settings. Extensive experiments on robotic manipulation benchmarks show that AC^2-VLA achieves up to a 1.79\times speedup while reducing FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense baseline, with comparable task success.

keyInsights: VLA inference contains structured redundancies (temporal, spatial, depth-wise) that can be exploited for computational efficiency.
The robot's "action context" (previous action state, current manipulation intent) is a critical, underutilized signal for adaptively allocating computational resources.
A unified framework that coordinates temporal reuse, spatial token pruning, and depth-wise layer skipping, guided by action context, yields superior efficiency compared to isolated approaches.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art VLA models for robotic manipulation incur high computational costs and inference latency, hindering real-time, closed-loop deployment.
Large multimodal backbones are executed repeatedly at every control step, reducing control frequency and responsiveness in dynamic environments.
Existing efficiency strategies often base compute allocation solely on visual cues, which don't always correlate with actual control difficulty or task-relevant reasoning.
results: AC^2-VLA achieved up to a 1.79× wall-clock speedup and reduced FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense CogACT baseline.
The framework improved the average task success rate to 76.8% on Google Robot Visual Matching, exceeding the dense baseline (74.8%) and other state-of-the-art VLA models.

It consistently outperformed other efficiency methods across benchmarks, demonstrating a superior speed-accuracy trade-off, and ablation studies confirmed the synergistic benefits of its unified components, particularly improved robustness via temporal consistency from cache reuse.
solution: An "action-prior router" is introduced to dynamically allocate computation by predicting unified gates for efficiency mechanisms.
The router utilizes an "action-centric context," fusing the previous action state, vision features, and language instruction, as the primary signal for compute allocation.

The framework unifies three complementary efficiency strategies: cognition caching (temporal reuse), action-context-aware token pruning (spatial sparsification), and conditional layer skipping (depth-wise execution).
paper_summary.summary: AC^2-VLA is an action-context-aware adaptive computation framework designed for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to enhance efficiency in robotic manipulation. It achieves up to a 1.79× wall-clock speedup and reduces FLOPs to 29.4% of the dense baseline, improving task success rates to 76.8% on Google Robot Visual Matching while coordinating temporal reuse, token pruning, and layer skipping.

title: AC^2-VLA: Action-Context-Aware Adaptive Computation in Vision-Language-Action Models for Efficient Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pretrained on large-scale multimodal datasets have emerged as powerful foundations for robotic perception and control. However, their massive scale, often billions of parameters, poses significant challenges for real-time deployment, as inference becomes computationally expensive and latency-sensitive in dynamic environments. To address this, we propose Token Expand-and-Merge-VLA (TEAM-VLA), a training-free token compression framework that accelerates VLA inference while preserving task performance. TEAM-VLA introduces a dynamic token expansion mechanism that identifies and samples additional informative tokens in the spatial vicinity of attention-highlighted regions, enhancing contextual completeness. These expanded tokens are then selectively merged in deeper layers under action-aware guidance, effectively reducing redundancy while maintaining semantic coherence. By coupling expansion and merging within a single feed-forward pass, TEAM-VLA achieves a balanced trade-off between efficiency and effectiveness, without any retraining or parameter updates. Extensive experiments on LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that TEAM-VLA consistently improves inference speed while maintaining or even surpassing the task success rate of full VLA models. The code is public available on \href{this https URL}{this https URL}

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Combining early-layer token expansion and context sampling with mid-layer token merging proves more effective than single-stage or direct pruning methods for VLA models.
Reconstructing coherent foreground regions through a density-based token expansion mechanism is crucial for overcoming the sparsity of initial image-text similarity-based pruning.
Action-guided merging in deeper layers effectively aggregates information by prioritizing relevance to both text and action tokens, outperforming simple pruning by preserving richer semantic context.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face computational bottlenecks and high latency due to their scale and Transformer architecture, hindering real-time robotic deployment.
Existing token compression methods often require additional training, rely on temporal cues (increasing memory), or produce fragmented foreground regions during early pruning.
Many latency-sensitive robotic tasks, such as closed-loop control and dynamic manipulation, demand low inference latency that current VLA models struggle to provide efficiently.
results: Achieved over 1.5x inference speedup, reducing the average CUDA latency of the OpenVLA-OFT model from 109 ms to 72.1 ms.
Maintained or improved task success rates, achieving an average of 96.6% across the LIBERO benchmark tasks, matching the baseline OpenVLA-OFT model.

Demonstrated a superior efficiency-performance trade-off compared to prior state-of-the-art methods, notably outperforming EfficientVLA by 7.7% success rate with marginal latency increase, all while being training-free and buffer-free.
solution: TEAM-VLA introduces a training-free, observation-only, dual-stage token compression framework that operates on current visual observations without relying on historical data.
It employs early-layer token pruning using `Context Sampling` and `Similarity-driven Token Expanding` to identify and reconstruct coherent foreground regions from sparse visual cues.
An `Action-guided Soft Bipartite Merging` strategy is applied in mid-layers of the VLA backbone to further compress tokens while preserving essential semantic and action-related information.
paper_summary.summary: A training-free token compression framework, TEAM-VLA, accelerates Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, achieving over 1.5x inference speedup with a latency of 72.1 ms. This framework maintains or improves task success rates, reaching 96.6% on the LIBERO benchmark, by intelligently combining early-layer token expansion with action-guided mid-layer token merging.

title: Token Expand-Merge: Training-Free Token Compression for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for
general-purpose robot control through natural language instructions. However,
