their high inference cost-stemming from large-scale token computation and
autoregressive decoding-poses significant challenges for real-time deployment
and edge applications. While prior work has primarily focused on architectural
optimization, we take a different perspective by identifying a dual form of
redundancy in VLA models: (i) high similarity across consecutive action steps,
and (ii) substantial redundancy in visual tokens. Motivated by these
observations, we propose FlashVLA, the first training-free and plug-and-play
acceleration framework that enables action reuse in VLA models. FlashVLA
improves inference efficiency through a token-aware action reuse mechanism that
avoids redundant decoding across stable action steps, and an information-guided
visual token selection strategy that prunes low-contribution tokens. Extensive
experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that FlashVLA reduces FLOPs by 55.7%
and latency by 36.0%, with only a 0.7% drop in task success rate. These results
demonstrate the effectiveness of FlashVLA in enabling lightweight, low-latency
VLA inference without retraining.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: VLA models exhibit temporal redundancy in action generation, where consecutive action outputs often show minimal changes, suggesting frequent recomputation is unnecessary.
Spatial redundancy exists in visual token processing, as a substantial portion of visual tokens contribute minimally to the model's decision-making process.
Significant inference efficiency gains can be achieved by exploiting these inherent redundancies without requiring model retraining or architectural modifications.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, despite their capabilities, suffer from high computational demands due to large Transformer architectures and extensive token processing.
The quadratic complexity of these models, processing hundreds of visual and language tokens, creates a significant bottleneck for real-time control and deployment on resource-constrained hardware.
Current state-of-the-art VLA models like OpenVLA have billions of parameters, making inference inefficient for practical robotic applications.
results: Achieved a 55.7% reduction in visual-token FLOPs (from 1.31e12 to 0.58e12) and a 36.0% reduction in inference latency (from 82.7ms to 52.9ms).
Maintained task success rate with only a 0.7% average drop on the LIBERO simulation benchmark (from 74.4% to 73.7%).
Produced smoother and more stable robot motions compared to the baseline, suggesting improved control consistency despite reduced computational load.
solution: Introduces FLASHVLA, a training-free acceleration framework based on a 'Think Twice, Act Once' paradigm.
Employs a FlashTrigger module that decides whether to reuse the previous action or perform a computationally reduced inference based on action stability and environmental changes.
Utilizes an Information Contribution Score (ICS) for intelligent visual token selection during pruned inference, enabling efficient token pruning compatible with Flash Attention.
paper_summary.summary: FLASHVLA introduces a training-free acceleration framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, reducing visual-token FLOPs by 55.7% and inference latency by 36.0% while maintaining task success rate with only a 0.7% average drop on the LIBERO benchmark.

title: Think Twice, Act Once: Token-Aware Compression and Action Reuse for  Efficient Inference in Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract:
Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, leveraging powerful pre trained
Vision-Language Models (VLMs) backends, have shown promise in robotic control
due to their impressive generalization ability. However, the success comes at a
cost. Their reliance on VLM backends with billions of parameters leads to high
computational costs and inference latency, limiting the testing scenarios to
mainly quasi-static tasks and hindering performance in dynamic tasks requiring
rapid interactions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes HiRT, a
Hierarchical Robot Transformer framework that enables flexible frequency and
performance trade-off. HiRT keeps VLMs running at low frequencies to capture
temporarily invariant features while enabling real-time interaction through a
high-frequency vision-based policy guided by the slowly updated features.
Experiment results in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate
significant improvements over baseline methods. Empirically, in static tasks,
we double the control frequency and achieve comparable success rates.
Additionally, on novel real-world dynamic ma nipulation tasks which are
challenging for previous VLA models, HiRT improves the success rate from 48% to
75%.

keyInsights: A hierarchical approach effectively integrates powerful but slow large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with fast, lightweight policies, enabling high-frequency control without sacrificing generalization.
Asynchronous operation, coupled with a training strategy robust to time-inconsistent VLM guidance, is critical for deploying VLA models in real-time, dynamic robotic environments.
Leveraging a VLM's rich visual scene understanding through latent conditioning significantly improves a smaller policy's generalization and multi-task learning capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models based on large VLMs suffer from high computational cost and inference latency, leading to low control frequencies.
These latency issues limit VLA models primarily to quasi-static manipulation tasks, making them unsuitable for dynamic tasks requiring rapid interactions and real-time control.
There is a need to balance the high-level understanding and generalization capabilities of large VLMs with the practical requirement for high-frequency, real-time action generation in robotics.
results: Achieved an inference speed of 9.8Hz, nearly doubling Vanilla-VLA's 4.1Hz, while maintaining comparable or superior success rates in quasi-static tasks (e.g., Metaworld: 76.4%; Franka-Kitchen: 80.8%).
Demonstrated an average 75.0% success rate in real-world dynamic manipulation tasks, significantly outperforming baselines like Vanilla-VLA (48.0%), RT-1* (18.0%), and Diffusion Policy (18.0%).

Ablation studies confirmed the importance of VLM-latent conditioning, multiple visual inputs for the execution module, and a training strategy that samples past observations for VLM input to ensure robustness during asynchronous deployment.
solution: Introduces HiRT (Hierarchical Robot Transformer), a hierarchical imitation learning framework inspired by the dual-process theory of human cognition.

Utilizes an "Understanding Module" (System 2) based on a large VLM (InstructBLIP) for low-frequency, long-term scene understanding and planning, and an "Execution Module" (System 1), a lightweight visual-based policy, for high-frequency, rapid action generation.

Employs asynchronous operation during inference, where the fast execution module uses cached VLM latents while the VLM updates in parallel at a lower frequency, and trains the policy with randomly sampled past observations to handle time-inconsistent latents.
paper_summary.summary: HiRT, a hierarchical framework from Tsinghua University and UC Berkeley, enables Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to perform dynamic robotic manipulation tasks by nearly doubling their inference speed to 9.8Hz while improving generalization. It achieves a 75% success rate in real-world dynamic tasks, significantly enhancing the practical applicability of VLA models beyond static environments.

title: HiRT: Enhancing Robotic Control with Hierarchical Robot Transformers

abstract: Developing efficient Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies is crucial for practical robotics deployment, yet current approaches face prohibitive computational costs and resource requirements. Existing diffusion-based VLA policies require multi-billion-parameter models and massive datasets to achieve strong performance. We tackle this efficiency challenge with two contributions: intermediate-modality fusion, which reallocates capacity to the diffusion head by pruning up to $50\%$ of LLM layers, and action-specific Global-AdaLN conditioning, which cuts parameters by $20\%$ through modular adaptation. We integrate these advances into a novel 950 M-parameter VLA called FLOWER. Pretrained in just 200 H100 GPU hours, FLOWER delivers competitive performance with bigger VLAs across $190$ tasks spanning ten simulation and real-world benchmarks and demonstrates robustness across diverse robotic embodiments. In addition, FLOWER achieves a new SoTA of 4.53 on the CALVIN ABC benchmark. Demos, code and pretrained weights are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Intermediate modality fusion provides an optimal balance between VLM semantic richness and computational efficiency for robotic manipulation, outperforming early and late fusion strategies.

The novel Action-Space Global-AdaLN-Zero mechanism can significantly reduce the parameter count of the transformer head while maintaining performance, by sharing modulation weights across layers for action-type conditioning.

Pre-trained Vision-Language Models emphasizing object detection and visual grounding, such as Florence-2-L, are more effective as backbones for robotic manipulation tasks compared to general reasoning models.
originalProblem: Existing generalist Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robot policies are computationally prohibitive, demanding multi-billion parameters, massive pretraining compute (e.g., tens of thousands of GPU hours), and high VRAM.
The high resource requirements of current VLAs create significant barriers to entry for diverse research communities and restrict practical real-world deployment on commodity hardware.
Current VLA architectures struggle to effectively balance rich semantic understanding from large pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with efficient and accurate action generation.
results: FLOWER achieved a new State-of-the-Art (SoTA) performance of 4.53 average sequence length on the CALVIN ABC benchmark, surpassing OpenVLA and ",0.

The policy demonstrated a 99% reduction in pretraining compute, requiring only 200 GPU hours compared to tens of thousands for models like OpenVLA and RDT-1B, while achieving competitive performance across 190 tasks.

In real-world evaluations, FLOWER doubled the average success rate of the second-best baseline (OpenVLA) across 20 distinct tasks (61% vs. 31%) and showed robust generalization to novel objects and challenging environmental conditions.
solution: Develops an efficient 950M parameter VLA policy using intermediate modality fusion, which prunes VLM layers and conditions the Flow Transformer on intermediate VLM embeddings for semantic richness with reduced overhead.

Introduces a cross-action space Flow Transformer leveraging Action-Space Global-AdaLN-Zero to efficiently handle heterogeneous action spaces with 20% fewer parameters, augmented by LoRA adapters for fine-grained modulation.
Utilizes Rectified Flow for action generation, enabling high-quality action sequences with fewer denoising steps and improved inference efficiency, crucial for low-latency control.
paper_summary.summary: FLOWER presents an efficient Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy for generalist robotics, achieving competitive or superior performance on 190 tasks across 10 benchmarks. It accomplishes this with a 99% reduction in pretraining compute and significantly lower memory requirements compared to prior large models, by incorporating intermediate modality fusion and a cross-action space Flow Transformer.

title: FLOWER: Democratizing Generalist Robot Policies with Efficient Vision-Language-Action Flow Policies

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant potential in real-world robotic manipulation. However, pre-trained VLA policies still suffer from substantial performance degradation during downstream deployment. Although fine-tuning can mitigate this issue, its reliance on costly demonstration collection and intensive computation makes it impractical in real-world settings. In this work, we introduce VLA-Pilot, a plug-and-play inference-time policy steering method for zero-shot deployment of pre-trained VLA without any additional fine-tuning or data collection. We evaluate VLA-Pilot on six real-world downstream manipulation tasks across two distinct robotic embodiments, encompassing both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that VLA-Pilot substantially boosts the success rates of off-the-shelf pre-trained VLA policies, enabling robust zero-shot generalization to diverse tasks and embodiments. Experimental videos and code are available at: this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong Center for Logistics Robotics

keyInsights: Pre-trained VLA policies often contain sufficient latent knowledge for task execution, and deployment failures typically stem from reliable action selection/execution rather than inherent skill inefficiency.

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can effectively serve as versatile, open-world verifiers and self-critics for robotic tasks, overcoming generalization limitations of traditional task-specific verifiers.

Combining the generative capabilities of diffusion models with the optimization power of evolutionary search enables active refinement of action proposals, moving beyond simple selection to generate improved, task-aligned actions.
originalProblem: Pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies suffer significant performance degradation when deployed in real-world, downstream tasks due to distribution shifts or visual distractors.
Fine-tuning VLA models for specific tasks is impractical due to high costs, intensive computational resources, and the risk of overfitting, which compromises generalist capabilities.

Existing inference-time policy steering methods are limited by poor generalization of their verifiers and an inability to optimize or generate new actions beyond fixed, potentially suboptimal, proposals.
results: Achieved significant manipulation success rate improvements: an average of +0.31 for DiVLA and +0.30 for RDT-1B across six downstream manipulation tasks.
Outperformed state-of-the-art steering baselines (V-GPS and FOREWARN) on out-of-distribution tasks, achieving an average MSR of 0.50 compared to 0.12 and 0.19 respectively.
Demonstrated performance comparable to, and sometimes exceeding, supervised fine-tuning of VLA policies using 50 expert demonstrations.
Exhibited robust zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization when deployed on a Franka Panda robot, yielding notable performance gains over baseline VLA policies.
solution: Introduces VLA-Pilot, a training-free, plug-and-play inference-time policy steering method that enhances the generalization and task alignment of pre-trained VLA policies.

Utilizes an Embodied Policy Steering Chain-of-Thought (EPS-CoT) module, powered by MLLMs like GPT-4o, to infer task-aligned steering objective rewards without requiring task-specific training, enabling open-world verification.

Develops an Evolutionary Diffusion algorithm that optimizes action proposals by iteratively refining and evolving them towards task alignment, using a truncated diffusion-denoising process to keep actions within the VLA policy's data manifold.
Incorporates an Iterative Steering Refinement mechanism for closed-loop correction, where the MLLM reflects on executed actions and regenerates rewards if inconsistencies are detected.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-Pilot, an inference-time policy steering method, enables zero-shot deployment of pre-trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for open-world objective reasoning and an evolutionary diffusion process for action optimization. The approach by researchers from The Chinese University of Hong Kong and Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia boosts manipulation success rates by an average of 30-31% and demonstrates robust generalization across diverse tasks and robot embodiments, matching or exceeding fine-tuning performance.

title: Towards Deploying VLA without Fine-Tuning: Plug-and-Play Inference-Time VLA Policy Steering via Embodied Evolutionary Diffusion

abstract: In this work, we introduce HoloBrain-0, a comprehensive Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that bridges the gap between foundation model research and reliable real-world robot deployment. The core of our system is a novel VLA architecture that explicitly incorporates robot embodiment priors, including multi-view camera parameters and kinematic descriptions (URDF), to enhance 3D spatial reasoning and support diverse embodiments. We validate this design through a scalable ``pre-train then post-train" paradigm, achieving state-of-the-art results on simulation benchmarks such as RoboTwin 2.0, LIBERO, and GenieSim, as well as strong results on challenging long-horizon real-world manipulation tasks. Notably, our efficient 0.2B-parameter variant rivals significantly larger baselines, enabling low-latency on-device deployment. To further accelerate research and practical adoption, we fully open-source the entire HoloBrain ecosystem, which includes: (1) powerful pre-trained VLA foundations; (2) post-trained checkpoints for multiple simulation suites and real-world tasks; and (3) RoboOrchard, a full-stack VLA infrastructure for data curation, model training and deployment. Together with standardized data collection protocols, this release provides the community with a complete, reproducible path toward high-performance robotic manipulation.

keyInsights: Explicitly embedding robot embodiment priors into VLA architectures significantly enhances 3D spatial reasoning and facilitates robust generalization across diverse robotic platforms.

Designing VLA models with a focus on efficiency, such as lightweight variants and optimized inference strategies, is crucial for bridging the gap between research and practical, real-time robot deployment.

An iterative, test-driven data curation methodology that focuses on proactive state expansion and targeted failure recovery can substantially reduce data requirements for complex, long-horizon tasks while maintaining high performance.
originalProblem: VLA agents often lack robust generalization to out-of-distribution states, visual perturbations, and physical variations in real-world environments.
Large foundation models typically incur significant inference latency, hindering their practical deployment for real-time robot control due to high computational demands.
Acquiring and curating large volumes of high-quality, expert-level robotic demonstrations for complex, long-horizon tasks is prohibitively expensive and inefficient.
results: The lightweight 0.2B-parameter HoloBrain-0-GD achieved a 90.8% success rate on RoboTwin 2.0 and a 74.0% average score on zero-shot LIBERO-Plus, outperforming larger existing VLA models.

HoloBrain-0 models surpassed the π0.5 baseline by average success rates of 5.65% to 8.02% across 10 real-world tasks, with improvements of 25% and 30% on challenging cloth and box folding tasks respectively.

The SimpleRTC and Teacher Forcing strategies enabled smooth asynchronous inference, eliminating inference pauses and achieving fluid control for complex dynamic tasks with negligible impact on efficiency.
solution: HoloBrain-0 employs an end-to-end VLA architecture that explicitly integrates robot embodiment priors, such as multi-view camera parameters and kinematic descriptions, to enhance 3D spatial reasoning.

The framework includes lightweight model variants (e.g., 0.2B parameters) and introduces SimpleRTC for smooth asynchronous inference, combined with a Teacher Forcing training strategy to ensure robust, low-latency on-device deployment.
A two-stage 'pre-train then post-train' data strategy is utilized, featuring an iterative, test-driven approach for quality-focused data curation, particularly for dexterous and long-horizon tasks.
paper_summary.summary: HoloBrain-0, a vision-language-action (VLA) foundation model by Horizon Robotics, introduces an integrated framework for robotic manipulation that incorporates explicit robot embodiment priors and a full-stack open-source ecosystem, RoboOrchard. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks with a lightweight 0.2B-parameter variant demonstrating comparable capabilities to larger models, enabling efficient on-device deployment for real-time control.

title: HoloBrain-0 Technical Report

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have advanced in robotic manipulation, yet practical deployment remains hindered by two key limitations: 1) perceptual redundancy, where irrelevant visual inputs are processed inefficiently, and 2) superficial instruction-vision alignment, which hampers semantic grounding of actions. In this paper, we propose SemanticVLA, a novel VLA framework that performs Semantic-Aligned Sparsification and Enhancement for Efficient Robotic Manipulation. Specifically: 1) To sparsify redundant perception while preserving semantic alignment, Semantic-guided Dual Visual Pruner (SD-Pruner) performs: Instruction-driven Pruner (ID-Pruner) extracts global action cues and local semantic anchors in SigLIP; Spatial-aggregation Pruner (SA-Pruner) compacts geometry-rich features into task-adaptive tokens in DINOv2. 2) To exploit sparsified features and integrate semantics with spatial geometry, Semantic-complementary Hierarchical Fuser (SH-Fuser) fuses dense patches and sparse tokens across SigLIP and DINOv2 for coherent representation. 3) To enhance the transformation from perception to action, Semantic-conditioned Action Coupler (SA-Coupler) replaces the conventional observation-to-DoF approach, yielding more efficient and interpretable behavior modeling for manipulation tasks. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world tasks show that SemanticVLA sets a new SOTA in both performance and efficiency. SemanticVLA surpasses OpenVLA on LIBERO benchmark by 21.1% in success rate, while reducing training cost and inference latency by 3.0-fold and this http URL is open-sourced and publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: Instruction-aware dual pruning of visual features from complementary encoders (SigLIP for semantics, DINOv2 for geometry) is essential for efficient and task-relevant perception in VLA models.
Hierarchical fusion, combining sparse semantic and dense geometric features through dynamic modulation, significantly improves both computational efficiency and the quality of visual representations.

Structuring action representations into semantically coherent motion primitives enhances the interpretability and efficiency of the observation-to-action mapping, moving beyond conventional 7-DoF discretization.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models process all observed pixels uniformly, leading to perceptual redundancy and inefficient computation by encoding irrelevant clutter.
Current VLA models rely on superficial cross-modal alignment, struggling to capture the complex semantic relations needed for fine-grained visual compositionality and precise manipulation.
These limitations hinder efficient computation, semantic grounding, and interpretability, impacting practical deployment in dynamic real-world environments.
results: SemanticVLA achieved the highest success rate of 97.7% on the LIBERO benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines like OpenVLA (76.5%) and OpenVLA-OFT (97.1%).
It demonstrated superior efficiency, reducing training cost by 3.0x (3.9h vs. 11.7h) and inference latency by 2.7x (0.089s vs. 0.240s) compared to OpenVLA.
In real-world experiments, SemanticVLA achieved a 77.8% success rate, outperforming OpenVLA-OFT (55.6%) by 22.2% on challenging long-horizon manipulation tasks.
solution: A Semantic-guided Dual Visual Pruner (SD-Pruner) filters irrelevant visual information using instruction-driven pruning for semantic grounding (SigLIP) and spatial aggregation for geometric details (DINOv2).

A Semantic-complementary Hierarchical Fuser (SH-Fuser) dynamically integrates sparse semantic features with dense geometric-rich features through layer-wise modulation, rather than simple concatenation.

A Semantic-conditioned Action Coupler (SA-Coupler) restructures action representation into semantically coherent tokens (3-DoF translation, 3-DoF rotation, 1-DoF gripper control) for efficient and interpretable action decoding.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, and Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab developed SemanticVLA, a framework for robotic manipulation that addresses perceptual redundancy and superficial instruction-vision alignment. It achieved a 97.7% success rate on the LIBERO benchmark, reducing training cost by 3.0x and inference latency by 2.7x compared to OpenVLA, while demonstrating robust performance in real-world tasks.

title: SemanticVLA: Semantic-Aligned Sparsification and Enhancement for Efficient Robotic Manipulation

abstract: The growing demand for real-time robotic deployment necessitates fast and on-device inference for vision-language-action (VLA) models. Within the VLA literature, efficiency has been extensively studied at the token level, such as visual token pruning. In contrast, systematic transformer layer reduction has received limited attention and, to the best of our knowledge, has not been explored for flow-based VLA models under knowledge distillation. In this work, we propose Shallow-pi, a principled knowledge distillation framework that aggressively reduces the transformer depth of both the VLM backbone and the flow-based action head, compressing the model from 18 to 6 layers. Shallow-pi achieves over two times faster inference with less than one percent absolute drop in success rate on standard manipulation benchmarks, establishing state-of-the-art performance among reduced VLA models. Crucially, we validate our approach through industrial-scale real-world experiments on Jetson Orin and Jetson Thor across multiple robot platforms, including humanoid systems, in complex and dynamic manipulation scenarios.

keyInsights: Transformer layer depth is a significantly more critical factor for inference latency improvements on both high-end and edge GPUs compared to reducing visual tokens.

Layer-skipping methods are unreliable and insufficient for flow-based VLA models due to the varying importance of layers across diffusion steps and the impracticality of maintaining full models in memory on edge devices.

A carefully designed attention distillation loss, specifically targeting action-to-vision-language cross-attention at a middle transformer layer, is crucial for effectively transferring knowledge and stabilizing training in aggressively compressed VLA student models.
originalProblem: Sophisticated flow-based VLA models are computationally expensive due to large VLM backbones, deep diffusion transformer action heads, and iterative inference steps, making real-time deployment on resource-constrained edge devices challenging.

Existing efficiency methods like token reduction or layer-skipping are often insufficient, workload-dependent, or impractical for flow-based VLAs, particularly for structurally compressing both the VLM and action head.
The inability to run these powerful models efficiently on robots leads to open-loop execution, hindering real-time adaptability and robustness in dynamic real-world environments.
results: Achieves up to 70% transformer layer reduction, resulting in over 2x faster CUDA inference time (e.g., 11.3ms vs. 25.5ms) with less than a 1% absolute drop in average success rate (e.g., 95% vs. 96%) on LIBERO benchmarks.
Outperforms prior state-of-the-art small-backbone and token compression VLA models in both simulation success rate and inference latency.

Demonstrates consistent improvements in real-world performance on ALOHA and RB-Y1 robots deployed on Jetson Orin/Thor, enabling faster reactions, reduced open-loop end-effector translation, and enhanced robustness to unseen perturbations in complex manipulation tasks.
solution: Shallow-π introduces a knowledge distillation (KD) framework to structurally reduce the transformer depth of both the VLM backbone and the flow-based action head in π-like VLA architectures.

The student model is initialized by uniformly subsampling layers from a pre-trained teacher model and is trained using a combined loss: a standard task loss, a knowledge distillation loss aligning student and teacher predicted velocities, and a novel attention distillation loss.

The attention distillation loss specifically targets the cross-attention between action queries and vision-language key-value pairs at a middle transformer layer, designed for the unique multimodal architecture of flow-based VLAs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Samsung Research present Shallow-π, a knowledge distillation framework that structurally reduces transformer layers in flow-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. This approach enables real-time deployment on edge robotic devices, achieving over 2x faster inference with less than a 1% absolute drop in success rate in complex real-world manipulation tasks.

title: Shallow-π: Knowledge Distillation for Flow-based VLAs

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have emerged as a promising approach to address the data scarcity challenge in robotics, enabling the development of generalizable visuomotor control policies. While models like OpenVLA showcase the potential of this paradigm, deploying large-scale VLMs on resource-constrained mobile manipulation systems remains a significant hurdle. This paper introduces Edge VLA (EVLA), a novel approach designed to significantly enhance the inference speed of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. EVLA maintains the representational power of these models while enabling real-time performance on edge devices. We achieve this through two key innovations: 1) Eliminating the autoregressive requirement for end-effector position prediction, leading to a 7x speedup in inference, and 2) Leveraging the efficiency of Small Language Models (SLMs), demonstrating comparable training performance to larger models with significantly reduced computational demands. Our early results demonstrate that EVLA achieves comparable training characteristics to OpenVLA while offering substantial gains in inference speed and memory efficiency. We release our model checkpoints and training \href{this https URL }{codebase} to foster further research.

keyInsights: Small Language Models (SLMs) can effectively maintain the representational power required for complex visuomotor control when integrated into a VLA framework, significantly reducing computational demands.

Autoregressive action prediction, a common pattern inherited from language models, is not essential for robotic end-effector control and its removal yields substantial inference speedups without compromising performance.
A carefully designed two-phase training strategy can successfully adapt pre-trained vision-language representations to efficient, real-time robotic action generation on edge hardware.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer strong generalization but are computationally too demanding and memory-intensive for deployment on resource-constrained mobile robotic systems.
The high inference latency of existing large VLA models prevents their use in real-time applications critical for dynamic robotic interaction on edge devices.
Traditional robotic control approaches often suffer from data scarcity, and while VLMs address this, their size limits practical deployment beyond powerful compute environments.
results: EdgeVLA achieves an inference time of 5 milliseconds (200 Hz), representing a 4x speedup compared to OpenVLA's 20ms on an A100-40GB GPU.
The model requires only 4 Gigabytes (GB) of memory, a 4x reduction compared to OpenVLA's 16 GB, making it suitable for deployment on edge devices like the Jetson Nano.
EVLA demonstrates comparable training characteristics (loss reduction and action token accuracy) to larger VLA models while achieving training iteration speeds that are approximately 7 times faster.
solution: Developed the EdgeVLA (EVLA) architecture, which utilizes a Small Language Model (Qwen2-0.5B) combined with pre-trained visual encoders (SigLIP and DINOv2).
Implemented a two-phase training strategy, starting with VLM pretraining for vision-language alignment, followed by fine-tuning for action generation using manipulation datasets.

Introduced a non-autoregressive approach for end-effector position prediction by removing the causal mask in the LLM, enabling simultaneous output of all action components rather than sequential generation.
paper_summary.summary: EdgeVLA addresses the challenge of deploying advanced Vision-Language-Action models on resource-constrained platforms, achieving real-time robotic control through the integration of Small Language Models and a non-autoregressive approach to end-effector prediction. This system delivers a substantial reduction in inference time and memory usage, making sophisticated mobile manipulation feasible on edge devices.

title: EdgeVLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract:
Recent progress in vision language foundation models has shown their ability
to understand multimodal data and resolve complicated vision language tasks,
including robotics manipulation. We seek a straightforward way of making use of
existing vision-language models (VLMs) with simple fine-tuning on robotics
data. To this end, we derive a simple and novel vision-language manipulation
framework, dubbed RoboFlamingo, built upon the open-source VLMs, OpenFlamingo.
Unlike prior works, RoboFlamingo utilizes pre-trained VLMs for single-step
vision-language comprehension, models sequential history information with an
explicit policy head, and is slightly fine-tuned by imitation learning only on
language-conditioned manipulation datasets. Such a decomposition provides
RoboFlamingo the flexibility for open-loop control and deployment on
low-performance platforms. By exceeding the state-of-the-art performance with a
large margin on the tested benchmark, we show RoboFlamingo can be an effective
and competitive alternative to adapt VLMs to robot control. Our extensive
experimental results also reveal several interesting conclusions regarding the
behavior of different pre-trained VLMs on manipulation tasks. We believe
RoboFlamingo has the potential to be a cost-effective and easy-to-use solution
for robotics manipulation, empowering everyone with the ability to fine-tune
their own robotics policy.

keyInsights: An explicit policy head (like LSTM or Transformer) dedicated to modeling sequential history is crucial for robot manipulation, as simply feeding historical frames into the VLM backbone is insufficient for robust temporal modeling.
Both strong vision-language representations from pre-training and targeted fine-tuning on robotic data are indispensable for high performance in imitation learning.
Instruction fine-tuning (IFT) in the underlying Large Language Models (LLMs) consistently improves policy performance, enhancing the robot's ability to follow diverse instructions.
originalProblem: Integrating large vision-language models (VLMs) directly into low-level robot control often requires extensive, costly co-fine-tuning on vast, often proprietary, datasets and models (e.g., RT-2).
Existing VLMs are primarily trained on static image-language pairs, making it challenging to adapt them efficiently for dynamic, sequential robot observations and continuous action prediction.

Many state-of-the-art VLM-driven robotics solutions are not widely accessible due to their high computational demands and reliance on private models and resources, hindering broader research and adoption.
results: RoboFlamingo significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods on the CALVIN benchmark, demonstrating a 2x performance improvement in average successful task sequence length.
It exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to novel visual environments and diverse linguistic variations (e.g., GPT-4 enriched instructions), improving robustness for real-world scenarios.
Ablation studies confirm the critical role of an explicit policy head for temporal modeling and the necessity of both VLM pre-training and targeted fine-tuning for effective robot imitation.
solution: RoboFlamingo leverages publicly accessible pre-trained VLMs (specifically OpenFlamingo) as a backbone for vision-language comprehension.

It employs a decoupled architecture with a VLM backbone for single-step multimodal understanding and a separate, explicit policy head (e.g., LSTM) to model sequential decision-making and generate robot actions.

The framework uses a highly efficient fine-tuning strategy, freezing the majority of the VLM backbone and only updating specific components (perceiver resampler, cross-attention layers) and the policy head using only language-conditioned manipulation datasets.
paper_summary.summary: RoboFlamingo presents a framework that adapts publicly available vision-language models for robot manipulation by employing an efficient fine-tuning strategy. The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on the CALVIN benchmark, demonstrating superior generalization while significantly reducing computational requirements.

title: Vision-Language Foundation Models as Effective Robot Imitators

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm in Embodied AI. However, the significant computational overhead of processing redundant visual tokens remains a critical bottleneck for real-time robotic deployment. While standard token pruning techniques can alleviate this, these task-agnostic methods struggle to preserve task-critical visual information. To address this challenge, simultaneously preserving both the holistic context and fine-grained details for precise action, we propose Compressor-VLA, a novel hybrid instruction-conditioned token compression framework designed for efficient, task-oriented compression of visual information in VLA models. The proposed Compressor-VLA framework consists of two token compression modules: a Semantic Task Compressor (STC) that distills holistic, task-relevant context, and a Spatial Refinement Compressor (SRC) that preserves fine-grained spatial details. This compression is dynamically modulated by the natural language instruction, allowing for the adaptive condensation of task-relevant visual information. Experimentally, extensive evaluations demonstrate that Compressor-VLA achieves a competitive success rate on the LIBERO benchmark while reducing FLOPs by 59% and the visual token count by over 3x compared to its baseline. The real-robot deployments on a dual-arm robot platform validate the model's sim-to-real transferability and practical applicability. Moreover, qualitative analyses reveal that our instruction guidance dynamically steers the model's perceptual focus toward task-relevant objects, thereby validating the effectiveness of our approach.

keyInsights: A hybrid compression architecture that balances global semantic understanding and local spatial precision is crucial for effective and efficient robotic manipulation.

Dynamically conditioning visual token compression on natural language instructions allows VLA models to intelligently focus on task-relevant visual cues, outperforming task-agnostic compression methods.

Reconstructive compression, rather than simple pruning, combined with instruction guidance, ensures the retention of essential visual information for precise action generation without aggressive data loss.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur substantial computational overhead (FLOPs and memory) due to high-capacity Vision Transformers, leading to significant inference latency.
Raw visual inputs often contain abundant redundant or task-irrelevant information, which wastes computational resources and can degrade the robot's policy quality.

Existing token reduction techniques are often task-agnostic, risking the loss of crucial task-critical visual information because they do not leverage language instructions to guide the compression process.
results: Achieved a 59% reduction in FLOPs and over 3x compression of visual tokens (from 512 to 160) while maintaining a highly competitive 97.3% average success rate on the LIBERO simulation benchmark, slightly surpassing OpenVLA-OFT's 97.1%.

Demonstrated robust real-world performance on a Mobile ALOHA robot, achieving 100% success on 'Spatial Awareness' tasks and 83.3% on 'Semantic Understanding' tasks, outperforming the OpenVLA-OFT baseline.

Qualitative analysis showed the STC dynamically shifts its attention to target objects based on changing language commands, and the SRC effectively preserves fine-grained details (e.g., object handles) critical for precise interaction.
solution: Replaces the standard projector layer in VLA models with a novel hybrid instruction-guided token compression framework, designed to reconstruct a compact set of task-relevant visual tokens.
Employs a dual-mechanism architecture consisting of a Semantic Task Compressor (STC) for global scene context and a Spatial Refinement Compressor (SRC) for preserving fine-grained local details.
Both STC and SRC dynamically modulate their compression and attention mechanisms using embeddings derived from the natural language instruction, ensuring task-aware information filtering.
paper_summary.summary: Compressor-VLA introduces an instruction-guided visual token compression framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, achieving a 59% reduction in FLOPs and 3x token compression while maintaining or improving task success rates on robotic manipulation benchmarks and real-world tasks. This enables more efficient real-time control for robots.

title: Compressor-VLA: Instruction-Guided Visual Token Compression for Efficient Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as a next-generation paradigm for robotics. We introduce dVLA, a diffusion-based VLA that leverages a multimodal chain-of-thought to unify visual perception, language reasoning, and robotic control in a single system. dVLA jointly optimizes perception, language understanding, and action under a single diffusion objective, enabling stronger cross-modal reasoning and better generalization to novel instructions and objects. For practical deployment, we mitigate inference latency by incorporating two acceleration strategies, a prefix attention mask and KV caching, yielding up to around times speedup at test-time inference. We evaluate dVLA in both simulation and the real world: on the LIBERO benchmark, it achieves state-of-the-art performance with a 96.4% average success rate, consistently surpassing both discrete and continuous action policies; on a real Franka robot, it succeeds across a diverse task suite, including a challenging bin-picking task that requires multi-step planning, demonstrating robust real-world performance. Together, these results underscore the promise of unified diffusion frameworks for practical, high-performance VLA robotics.

keyInsights: A single discrete diffusion objective effectively resolves gradient conflicts and architectural gaps across vision, language, and action modalities, leading to more coherent cross-modal learning in VLA systems.

Jointly generating multimodal Chain-of-Thought (visual subgoals and textual reasoning) alongside actions deepens the model's understanding of task decomposition and implicit physical laws, allowing it to predict consequences, including potential failures.

Practical inference efficiency for complex multimodal VLA models can be achieved through techniques like prefix attention masks and KV caching, enabling real-time robotic control with only marginal performance trade-offs.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face gradient conflicts and architectural disparities when co-training distinct modalities, hindering effective cross-modal learning.
Current VLA approaches struggle with open-world instructions and long-horizon tasks due to a lack of explicit intermediate reasoning and insufficient understanding of underlying physical laws.
Complex multimodal generation in VLA models leads to high inference latency, posing a significant barrier to their practical, real-time deployment in robotics.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art average success rates of 96.4% on the LIBERO simulation benchmark and 65% in real-world tasks (e.g., Bin Picking), outperforming all continuous and discrete action baselines.

Multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning enhanced dVLA's average success rate by 6.6 percentage points in simulation and 12.5 percentage points in real-world tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in complex scenarios.
Demonstrated ~2x inference speedup (from 1.3-1.5 Hz to 2.9-3 Hz) with minimal performance degradation (<1% drop in success rates) across tasks, making dVLA viable for real-time robotic deployment.
solution: Introduces dVLA, a unified discrete diffusion model that converts vision, language, and continuous actions into discrete tokens and optimizes them under a single diffusion objective.
Implements a multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) mechanism that jointly generates visual subgoals, textual reasoning, and action sequences by masking tokens across all modalities during training.
Integrates acceleration strategies, including a prefix attention mask and KV caching (dLLM-Cache), to significantly improve inference efficiency without substantial performance degradation.
paper_summary.summary: Developed by researchers from Midea Group, Peking University, and Shanghai Jiaotong University, dVLA unifies visual perception, language reasoning, and action generation within a single diffusion framework for robotic control. The model achieves state-of-the-art average success rates of 96.4% in simulation (LIBERO benchmark) and 65% in real-world robotic tasks, while also accelerating inference speed by approximately 2x.

title: dVLA: Diffusion Vision-Language-Action Model with Multimodal Chain-of-Thought

abstract: Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models built on pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in robotic manipulation. However, these models remain constrained by the single-frame image paradigm and fail to fully leverage the temporal information offered by multi-frame histories, as directly feeding multiple frames into VLM backbones incurs substantial computational overhead and inference latency. We propose CronusVLA, a unified framework that extends single-frame VLA models to the multi-frame paradigm. CronusVLA follows a two-stage process: (1) Single-frame pretraining on large-scale embodied datasets with autoregressive prediction of action tokens, establishing an effective embodied vision-language foundation; (2) Multi-frame post-training, which adapts the prediction of the vision-language backbone from discrete tokens to learnable features, and aggregates historical information via feature chunking. CronusVLA effectively addresses the existing challenges of multi-frame modeling while enhancing performance and observational robustness. To evaluate the robustness under temporal and spatial disturbances, we introduce SimplerEnv-OR, a novel benchmark featuring 24 types of observational disturbances and 120 severity levels. Experiments across three embodiments in simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that CronusVLA achieves leading performance and superior robustness, with a 70.9% success rate on SimplerEnv, a 26.8% improvement over OpenVLA on LIBERO, and the highest robustness score on SimplerEnv-OR. These results highlight the potential of efficient multi-frame adaptation in VLA models for more powerful and robust real-world deployment.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Efficient integration of multi-frame temporal information is crucial for enhancing the performance, fluidity, and robustness of robotic manipulation, moving beyond the limitations of instantaneous single-frame perception.

Decoupling single-frame perception from multi-frame temporal modeling allows for leveraging powerful pre-trained VLM backbones while achieving significant computational efficiency during multi-frame inference.

Rigorous evaluation of observational robustness against diverse disturbances is essential for bridging the gap between laboratory performance and the reliable deployment of VLA models in complex, unpredictable real-world environments.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily operate within a single-frame paradigm, failing to fully utilize temporal information critical for understanding motion cues and resolving state ambiguities.
Directly extending large-scale VLA models to a multi-frame paradigm incurs prohibitive computational overhead and inference latency due to the quadratic scaling of self-attention with input tokens.

Current robotic manipulation benchmarks often lack comprehensive evaluation for 'observational robustness,' leaving a gap in assessing how VLA models perform under real-world visual disturbances like blur, noise, or occlusions.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art average success rates of 70.9% on SimplerEnv Google Robot and WidowX Robot tasks, and 97.0% on the LIBERO benchmark, showing significant improvements (e.g., 40.3% over OpenVLA on LIBERO long-horizon tasks).

Demonstrated superior observational robustness on the SimplerEnv-OR benchmark, consistently outperforming prior methods under various temporal and spatial disturbances, with high R-Scores (e.g., 96.2 R-Score for Sparse 1:3 disturbances).

Maintained fast inference speeds (e.g., 8.7 Hz for the 7B model with 7 frames), addressing the computational challenges of multi-frame processing and making advanced temporal modeling practical for real-time robotic control.
solution: A two-stage framework: initial single-frame VLA pretraining to establish a strong vision-language foundation, followed by efficient multi-frame post-training on high-quality datasets.

Introduces continuous 'learnable features' from the VLA backbone and 'feature chunking' with a first-in, first-out (FIFO) queue to aggregate multi-frame historical information while reusing previously computed VLM features, ensuring fast inference.

Employs a DiT-based cross-frame decoder with a feature modulator to dynamically balance current and past frame contributions, and a multi-frame regularization technique using stop-gradients to efficiently update the VLA backbone without disrupting single-frame perception.
Developed SimplerEnv-OR, a novel benchmark with 24 types of temporal and spatial observational disturbances across over 120 severity levels, to quantitatively assess and compare model robustness.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Lab and collaborating institutions introduce CronusVLA, an efficient framework that extends single-frame Vision-Language-Action models to leverage multi-frame observations for robust robotic manipulation. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse tasks in simulation and real-world, significantly improving resilience against visual disturbances while maintaining fast inference speeds.

title: CronusVLA: Towards Efficient and Robust Manipulation via Multi-Frame Vision-Language-Action Modeling

abstract:
In recent years, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become a vital
research direction in robotics due to their impressive multimodal understanding
and generalization capabilities. Despite the progress, their practical
deployment is severely constrained by inference speed bottlenecks, particularly
in high-frequency and dexterous manipulation tasks. While recent studies have
explored Jacobi decoding as a more efficient alternative to traditional
autoregressive decoding, its practical benefits are marginal due to the lengthy
iterations. To address it, we introduce consistency distillation training to
predict multiple correct action tokens in each iteration, thereby achieving
acceleration. Besides, we design mixed-label supervision to mitigate the error
accumulation during distillation. Although distillation brings acceptable
speedup, we identify that certain inefficient iterations remain a critical
bottleneck. To tackle this, we propose an early-exit decoding strategy that
moderately relaxes convergence conditions, which further improves average
inference efficiency. Experimental results show that the proposed method
achieves more than 4 times inference acceleration across different baselines
while maintaining high task success rates in both simulated and real-world
robot tasks. These experiments validate that our approach provides an efficient
and general paradigm for accelerating multimodal decision-making in robotics.
Our project page is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Consistency training can enable autoregressively trained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to robustly predict multiple correct action tokens in parallel during Jacobi decoding, even from imperfect intermediate states.

Relaxing strict convergence criteria through early-exit decoding is viable for robotic tasks, as perfect action sequence convergence is not always necessary for success, and can significantly boost inference speed.
A mixed-label supervision strategy is essential during consistency distillation to maintain a VLA model's native manipulation skills and prevent performance degradation from teacher-generated errors.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a severe inference speed bottleneck, limiting their practical deployment in high-frequency, real-time robotic control and dexterous manipulation tasks.

Existing Jacobi decoding methods provide only marginal speedups (e.g., 1.28x) for autoregressively trained VLAs because these models struggle to generate accurate predictions when conditioned on the 'incorrect' intermediate tokens common in Jacobi iterations.
Certain 'inefficient iterations' in Jacobi decoding consume disproportionate time for marginal token updates, further reducing average decoding speed.
results: Achieves up to 4.1x inference acceleration (225.0 Tokens/s) and 4.3x frequency increase (25.60 Hz) on OpenVLA in simulation compared to baselines, while maintaining comparable success rates.

Demonstrates superior performance in real-world dexterous manipulation tasks, boosting control frequency to 13.0 Hz (a 3.9x increase) and significantly improving success rates (e.g., 80% on pour water, 75% on fold towel) compared to LLaVA-VLA.

Ablation studies confirm that consistency training and early-exit decoding are the primary drivers of acceleration, and the mixed-label supervision strategy is crucial for maintaining action accuracy.
solution: Develops CEED-VLA, which fine-tunes pre-trained VLA 'teacher models' into 'student models' using a novel consistency training procedure.

Applies consistency distillation by collecting Jacobi trajectories (intermediate states and final fixed points) and training the student model to directly map any intermediate state to the final fixed point in a single iteration.

Introduces 'mixed-label autoregressive supervision' to preserve native manipulation skills by selectively using either teacher-generated fixed points or ground-truth actions as supervisory labels, mitigating error accumulation during distillation.

Implements 'early-exit decoding' during inference, which prematurely halts the Jacobi decoding process at a predefined iteration, emitting the intermediate output to circumvent inefficient convergence steps.
paper_summary.summary: CEED-VLA introduces a framework to accelerate Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model inference by optimizing Jacobi decoding. It achieves up to 4.1x speedup in simulation and enables robust, high-frequency robotic control in real-world dexterous manipulation tasks.

title: CEED-VLA: Consistency Vision-Language-Action Model with Early-Exit  Decoding

abstract: Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown impressive flexibility and generalization, yet their deployment in robotic manipulation remains limited by heavy computational overhead and inference latency. In this work, we present ActDistill, a general action-guided self-derived distillation framework that transfers the action prediction capability of any existing VLA model to a lightweight counterpart. Unlike previous efficiency strategies that primarily emphasize vision-language correlations, ActDistill leverages action priors to guide knowledge transfer and model compression, achieving action-oriented efficiency for VLA models. Specifically, we employ a well-trained VLA model as the teacher and introduce a graph-structured encapsulation strategy to explicitly model the hierarchical evolution of action prediction. The student model, derived from the graph-encapsulated teacher, is further equipped with a dynamic router that adaptively selects computation paths based on action prediction demands, guided by hierarchical graph-informed supervision to ensure smooth and efficient evolution. During inference, graph-related auxiliary components are removed, allowing the student to execute only dynamically routed layers and predict high-precision actions with minimal computation and latency. Experiments on embodied benchmarks demonstrate that ActDistill achieves comparable or superior performance to full-scale VLA models while reducing computation by over 50% with up to 1.67 times speedup, thereby establishing a general paradigm toward efficient embodied intelligence.

keyInsights: Explicitly guiding knowledge distillation with action priors, rather than just vision-language correlations, is crucial for preserving precision and stability in VLA model compression.
Graph-structured encapsulation effectively identifies and abstracts decisive action-relevant relational information from dense representations, mitigating critical information attrition.

Dynamic routing enables adaptive computation, allowing the student model to skip unnecessary layers at inference time while maintaining performance, providing a flexible efficiency-accuracy trade-off.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from high computational cost and inference latency, limiting their deployment in real-time robotic applications.
Existing efficiency methods for VLA models often lead to "critical information attrition" and "action-semantic discontinuity," compromising precise action prediction.
Prior compression strategies, borrowed from Vision-Language Models (VLMs), are primarily cognition-centered and may not effectively preserve action-specific information.
results: ActDistill achieved an average task success rate of 73.95% on the LIBERO benchmark, only 1.0% lower than the full OpenVLA model, and even surpassed other baselines by 2.6% on the challenging LIBERO-Long suite.

The framework delivered up to a 1.67x speedup and over 55% FLOPs reduction on the SIMPLER benchmark (CogACT backbone) while maintaining or slightly improving task success rates (e.g., 0.45% increase on Variant Aggregation).

Ablation studies confirmed that Graph-Structured Encapsulation is critical, with its removal causing a significant 9.55% drop in success rate, and both semantic alignment and action consistency losses are complementary and essential for robust performance.
solution: ActDistill introduces a two-stage action-guided self-derived distillation framework: Graph-Structured Encapsulation and Action-Guided Self-Derived Distillation.
The teacher model uses Graph-Structured Encapsulation to dynamically identify and abstract action-relevant information into compact semantic capsules via attention-based graph message passing.

A lightweight student model, equipped with a dynamic router for adaptive layer execution, is trained to reconstruct the teacher's action-centric decision-making process using semantic alignment and action consistency losses, guided by action priors.
paper_summary.summary: ActDistill presents an action-guided self-derived distillation framework that significantly enhances the efficiency of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation by achieving up to a 1.67x speedup and over 50% FLOPs reduction while maintaining comparable or superior task success rates on embodied benchmarks.

title: ActDistill: General Action-Guided Self-Derived Distillation for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models equipped with Flow Matching (FM) action heads achieve state-of-the-art performance in complex robot manipulation. However, the multi-step iterative ODE solving required by FM introduces inference latency that precludes responsive physical control. While current acceleration efforts optimize the Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone, the action head bottleneck remains overlooked. To address this, we propose ProbeFlow, a training-free adaptive inference framework tai- lored for continuous robotic control. By evaluating geometric trajectory complexity via the cosine similarity between initial and lookahead velocity vectors, ProbeFlow dynamically sched- ules integration steps to prune redundant network evaluations. On the MetaWorld benchmark, it accelerates action decoding by 14.8x (reducing average steps from N = 50 to 2.6) and cuts end-to-end system latency by 2.8x without compromising the manipulation success rate. On the long-horizon LIBERO benchmark, the probe automatically allocates a denser schedule to navigate semantic bottlenecks, effectively resolving the flow solver delay. Real-world physical deployments confirm that ProbeFlow successfully mitigates action decoding latency while ensuring execution stability, offering a highly practical solution for low-latency continuous generative policies.

keyInsights: Generative probability paths in Flow Matching often exhibit varying degrees of linearity, allowing for significant computational savings by adaptively adjusting the numerical integration step count.

A simple, single-shot geometric probe (cosine similarity of velocities) can effectively and efficiently identify local path curvature without requiring additional training or complex higher-order derivative calculations.

By aligning computational cost with the local complexity of the trajectory, it is possible to drastically reduce inference latency in FM-based VLA models without compromising the precision required for robotic manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models relying on Flow Matching (FM) for action generation suffer from high inference latency, primarily due to the multi-step iterative ODE solving process.

Standard fixed-step ODE solvers for FM require a large number of steps (e.g., N=50) for sufficient accuracy in robotic control, leading to substantial computational waste in simple or linear trajectory regions.
Existing acceleration efforts for VLA models largely focus on optimizing the VLM backbone, leaving the iterative computational bottleneck of the FM action head unaddressed.
results: ProbeFlow reduced action head latency on MetaWorld by 14.8x (from 235.7 ms to 15.9 ms) with an average of 2.6 steps, maintaining an 83.2% success rate, comparable to the N=50 baseline (82.5%).
On the LIBERO benchmark, ProbeFlow achieved an 8.5x speedup (from 278.7 ms to 32.7 ms) with an average of 4.5 steps, yielding an 88.7% success rate.

Real-world deployment on a UFACTORY xArm7 for a "Pick-and-Place" task confirmed latency reduction to 12.26 ms (from 270.3 ms for N=50) with comparable success rates (7/10 trials), validating its practical viability.
solution: ProbeFlow introduces a training-free adaptive inference framework that dynamically adjusts the number of integration steps for the Flow Matching action head.
A one-shot Lookahead Linearity Probe is employed, which geometrically assesses trajectory complexity by comparing initial and projected velocities using cosine similarity.

Based on the linearity probe's score, the system either directly computes the final action state for highly linear paths (minimal steps) or triggers a denser integration schedule for complex, curved trajectories, maximizing state reuse.
paper_summary.summary: ProbeFlow, a training-free adaptive flow matching framework, tackles the inference latency in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by dynamically adjusting the number of ODE integration steps for action generation. It achieves up to a 14.8x speedup in action head inference on the MetaWorld benchmark while preserving manipulation task success rates, and demonstrates efficiency in real-world robot deployments.

title: ProbeFlow: Training-Free Adaptive Flow Matching for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a unified paradigm for robotic perception and control, enabling emergent generalization and long-horizon task execution. However, their deployment in dynamic, real-world environments is severely hin dered by high inference latency. While smooth robotic interaction requires control frequencies of 20 to 30 Hz, current VLA models typi cally operate at only 3-5 Hz on edge devices due to the memory bound nature of autoregressive decoding. Existing optimizations often require extensive retraining or compromise model accuracy. To bridge this gap, we introduce ActionFlow, a system-level inference framework tailored for resource-constrained edge plat forms. At the core of ActionFlow is a Cross-Request Pipelin ing strategy, a novel scheduler that redefines VLA inference as a macro-pipeline of micro-requests. The strategy intelligently batches memory-bound Decode phases with compute-bound Prefill phases across continuous time steps to maximize hardware utilization. Furthermore, to support this scheduling, we propose a Cross Request State Packed Forward operator and a Unified KV Ring Buffer, which fuse fragmented memory operations into efficient dense computations. Experimental results demonstrate that ActionFlow achieves a 2.55x improvement in FPS on the OpenVLA-7B model without retraining, enabling real-time dy namic manipulation on edge hardware. Our work is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The continuous nature of VLA tasks in robotics provides a natural opportunity for "micro-request" pipelining, enabling internal batching within a single stream to parallelize inherently serial autoregressive decoding.

The VLA inference bottleneck stems from memory-bound decode operations and thousands of small kernel launches, which can be significantly optimized by aggregating them into larger, compute-intensive operations.
Purely system-level optimizations, without modifying the VLA model's algorithm or requiring retraining, can provide substantial latency reductions crucial for real-time edge deployment.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, while powerful for embodied AI, incur high inference latency (3-5 Hz) on resource-constrained edge devices, preventing real-time dynamic robotic control which typically requires 10-30 Hz.
The primary bottleneck lies in the memory-bound, autoregressive decode phase of the LLM backbone, suffering from low arithmetic intensity and excessive kernel launch overheads on GPUs.
Existing LLM acceleration techniques often target multi-user server scenarios or introduce compromises like retraining or accuracy degradation when applied to VLA-specific edge applications.
results: ActionFlow delivered a 2.56x speedup on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin (3.20 FPS vs. 1.25 FPS) and 2.55x speedup on NVIDIA RTX 5090 (19.45 FPS vs. 7.62 FPS) for the OpenVLA-7B model, consistently outperforming the baseline.
Under demanding workloads with longer prefill and decode lengths, the framework achieved up to a 4.36x speedup and demonstrated significantly better robustness to increasing decode lengths.

The system maintained functional lossless performance, showing comparable task success rates on the LIBERO benchmark, confirming that acceleration does not degrade VLA model accuracy or robotic manipulation capabilities.
Custom-fused operators were essential, contributing an additional 18.5% to 24.7% performance improvement over a naive pipelining approach by minimizing synchronization overheads.
solution: Implements a Cross-Request Pipelining strategy that redefines VLA inference by overlapping the compute-intensive prefill phase of the current request with the memory-intensive decode phases of multiple historical requests.

Introduces the Cross-Request State (CRS) Packed Forward operator to aggregate inputs into a single tensor for a unified, compute-dense forward pass, shifting operations from memory-bound to compute-bound.

Deploys a Unified KV Ring Buffer with custom-fused kernels (FusedRoPEAndWriteKV, InPlaceKVShift) for contiguous and efficient Key-Value (KV) cache management across pipelined requests, eliminating synchronization bubbles.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Science and Technology of China developed ActionFlow, a system-level inference framework that achieves up to 4.36x speedup for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on edge devices without compromising functional correctness. This framework employs a cross-request pipelining strategy, custom-fused operators, and an efficient KV cache management to enable real-time robotic control.

title: ActionFlow: A Pipelined Action Acceleration for Vision Language Models on Edge

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have rapidly advanced embodied intelligence, enabling robots to execute complex, instruction-driven tasks. However, as model capacity and visual context length grow, the inference cost of VLA systems becomes a major bottleneck for real-world deployment on resource-constrained platforms. Existing visual token pruning methods mainly rely on semantic saliency or simple temporal cues, overlooking the continuous physical interaction, a fundamental property of VLA tasks. Consequently, current approaches often prune visually sparse yet structurally critical regions that support manipulation, leading to unstable behavior during early task phases. To overcome this, we propose a shift toward an explicit Interaction-First paradigm. Our proposed \textbf{training-free} method, VLA-IAP (Interaction-Aligned Pruning), introduces a geometric prior mechanism to preserve structural anchors and a dynamic scheduling strategy that adapts pruning intensity based on semantic-motion alignment. This enables a conservative-to-aggressive transition, ensuring robustness during early uncertainty and efficiency once interaction is locked. Extensive experiments show that VLA-IAP achieves a \textbf{97.8\% success rate} with a \textbf{$1.25\times$ speedup} on the LIBERO benchmark, and up to \textbf{$1.54\times$ speedup} while maintaining performance \textbf{comparable to the unpruned backbone}. Moreover, the method demonstrates superior and consistent performance across multiple model architectures and three different simulation environments, as well as a real robot platform, validating its strong generalization capability and practical applicability. Our project website is: \href{this https URL}{this http URL}.

keyInsights: Prioritizing physical interaction and geometric information over purely semantic saliency in visual token pruning is crucial for robust embodied AI, rectifying a common bias in existing VLM-based approaches.

Explicitly preserving structural anchors (e.g., edges) through a lightweight geometric prior can prevent the premature loss of critical visual cues necessary for fine manipulation, irrespective of their semantic richness.

Dynamically adjusting pruning intensity based on the alignment between semantic intent and physical motion allows for adaptive resource allocation, ensuring stability in uncertain phases and maximum efficiency during confident interaction.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high computational costs and latency due to processing long visual sequences, hindering real-time robotic deployment.
Current visual token pruning methods exhibit a "Perception-First" bias, prematurely discarding visually sparse but geometrically critical regions essential for precise manipulation.
Pruning mechanisms that rely on a model's internal attention scores inherit and amplify deficiencies if the pre-trained VLA model lacks sufficient physical grounding.
results: VLA-IAP achieved an average success rate of 97.8% on LIBERO with a 1.25x inference speedup, surpassing the unpruned OpenVLA-OFT baseline's 97.1%.

Under aggressive 30% token retention, VLA-IAP maintained a 33.3% success rate on the challenging VLABench, significantly outperforming perception-first methods that experienced catastrophic failures (e.g., 6.7% for DivPrune).

Real-world robotic deployments demonstrated latency reductions of 1.48x for single-arm and 1.47x for dual-arm tasks, with an overall average success rate increase of 4.0% across various manipulation tasks.
solution: A "Training-Free" VLA-IAP framework that re-conceptualizes visual compression as dynamic affordance extraction, integrating explicit physical interaction knowledge into pruning.

Multi-dimensional physical priors are constructed, including a geometric prior (edge enhancement), a semantic prior (text-visual alignment), and a motion prior (temporal feature changes) to identify critical visual tokens.

An interaction-aligned dynamic strategy adaptively switches between conservative and aggressive pruning modes based on the semantic-motion alignment (IoU), ensuring robustness during exploration and efficiency during established interaction.
paper_summary.summary: VLA-IAP introduces a training-free visual token pruning framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, employing an "Interaction-First" paradigm to enhance efficiency and robustness in robotic manipulation. It achieves up to a 1.54x inference speedup, while maintaining or improving task success rates by preserving crucial geometric and motion cues.

title: VLA-IAP: Training-Free Visual Token Pruning via Interaction Alignment for Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a pivotal approach to learning robotic manipulation at scale by repurposing large pre-trained Vision-Language-Models (VLM) to output robotic actions. However, adapting VLMs for robotic domains comes with an unnecessarily high computational cost, which we attribute to the tokenization scheme of visual inputs. In this work, we aim to enable efficient VLA training by proposing Oat-VLA, an Object-Agent-centric Tokenization for VLAs. Building on the insights of object-centric representation learning, our method introduces an inductive bias towards scene objects and the agent's own visual information. As a result, we find that Oat-VLA can drastically reduce the number of visual tokens to just a few tokens without sacrificing performance. We reveal that Oat-VLA converges at least twice as fast as OpenVLA on the LIBERO suite, as well as outperform OpenVLA in diverse real-world pick and place tasks.

keyInsights: Drastically reducing visual tokens from 256 to 16 (a 93.75% decrease) significantly enhances VLA training efficiency, enabling faster convergence and larger batch sizes.
Introducing an inductive bias by explicitly focusing on objects and the agent's gripper provides more semantically relevant visual input, improving learning efficacy for manipulation tasks.
The modular design of Oat-VLA allows it to leverage existing VLA checkpoints and their pre-trained knowledge, modifying only the visual tokenization frontend without altering the LLM backbone.
originalProblem: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models incur high computational costs due to dense patch-based visual tokenization, which processes an excessive number of visual tokens.
The substantial compute requirements for VLA training limit accessibility for many research groups, hindering broader participation and progress in the field.
Current VLA visual processing often includes redundant background information, leading to inefficiencies as not all visual input is equally relevant for robotic manipulation tasks.
results: Oat-VLA achieves more than a 2x speedup in VLA training time and steps compared to OpenVLA, processing over twice as many examples per second due to reduced memory requirements and larger batch sizes.

The method consistently matches or slightly outperforms OpenVLA across all four LIBERO benchmark task suites and shows a significant performance advantage (52% vs. 72% in-distribution, 29% vs. 46% out-of-distribution) in real-world pick and place tasks.

Qualitative assessments and quantitative results on real-world tasks indicate that Oat-VLA enables greater precision in object manipulation, with fewer mis-grasps or incorrect placements compared to the baseline.
solution: Oat-VLA implements a novel visual tokenization scheme that selectively extracts object-centric and agent-centric visual information instead of using dense image patches.
Unsupervised object extraction (FT-Dinosaur) is used to generate a fixed number of object masks, with average pooling applied to corresponding visual patches to create compact object-centric tokens.

A dedicated gripper detector identifies the robot's end-effector, and a fixed grid of high-resolution visual patches is selected around this location to form agent-centric tokens, preserving crucial manipulation details.
paper_summary.summary: Oat-VLA introduces an object-agent-centric tokenization method for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, reducing visual tokens by 93.75% and achieving a 2x training speedup. This approach maintains or improves performance on robotic manipulation benchmarks and demonstrates increased robustness in real-world tasks.

title: Focusing on What Matters: Object-Agent-centric Tokenization for Vision Language Action models

abstract: Robotic manipulation with Vision-Language-Action models requires efficient inference over long-horizon multi-modal context, where attention to dense visual tokens dominates computational cost. Existing methods optimize inference speed by reducing visual redundancy within VLA models, but they overlook the varying redundancy across robotic manipulation stages. We observe that the visual token redundancy is higher in coarse manipulation phase than in fine-grained operations, and is strongly correlated with the action dynamic. Motivated by this observation, we propose \textbf{A}ction-aware \textbf{D}ynamic \textbf{P}runing (\textbf{ADP}), a multi-modal pruning framework that integrates text-driven token selection with action-aware trajectory gating. Our method introduces a gating mechanism that conditions the pruning signal on recent action trajectories, using past motion windows to adaptively adjust token retention ratios in accordance with dynamics, thereby balancing computational efficiency and perceptual precision across different manipulation stages.
